title: JFrog Learn - Home
body: Skip to content Your personal JFrog knowledge base · built bottom-up Everything you need to master Artifactory & Xray . A growing, self-paced learning hub. Read the Fundamentals first if you're new - it assumes nothing - then jump into focused deep-dives on replication, distribution, automation, security, and platform operations. New topics get added here over time. Start with the fundamentals Search the knowledge base Ask the docs 9 topic pages 3 lab tracks 0 assumptions How to use this site Press / or ⌘K anywhere to search every page, or use Ask the docs to chat with a custom NotebookLM model built from these pages. Tip Every page links to primary JFrog documentation for the details. This site is the map and the mental models; the official docs are the full reference. Start here 📦 Foundations Fundamentals The problem these tools solve, the core vocabulary, the 4 repository types, Xray scanning, policies & watches, and three hands-on lab tracks. The bedrock everything else builds on. ~30 min · concepts + labs Core concepts 🔁 Multi-site Replication & Federation Push/pull replication vs. federated repositories. How to keep artifacts in sync across data centers and JPDs, and when to choose each approach. Deep-dive ⬆️ Lifecycle Build promotion in practice Move a build through dev → staging → release without rebuilding. build-info, status changes, copy vs. move, and the CLI/REST commands that do it. Deep-dive + CLI Distribution 🎁 Software supply chain Release Bundles & Distribution Immutable, signed collections of artifacts. How Distribution ships them to edge nodes and air-gapped sites, with provenance you can verify. Deep-dive Automation & security 🔌 Integration Artifactory REST API Authentication, the most useful endpoints, and how to drive them from curl and the JFrog CLI. Automate repo creation, user management, and more. Reference + examples 🐸 Shift-left Frogbot & IDE integration The Git bot that scans pull requests with Xray and opens fix PRs automatically. Plus the IDE plugins that flag vulnerabilities as you code. How-to + YAML 🚦 CI/CD JFrog Pipelines The YAML-based CI/CD engine. Integrations, resources, steps, and pipeline sources - build a working pipeline from a "Hello World" example up. How-to + YAML 🔑 Security Access tokens & permissions The full identity model: users, groups, permission targets, and scoped access tokens. How to grant least-privilege access the right way. Deep-dive + CLI Platform operations ☸️ K8s Kubernetes / Helm setup Two angles: installing Artifactory itself on Kubernetes via the JFrog Helm charts, and using Artifactory as your Helm chart registry for helm pull / push . How-to + CLI A self-paced learning hub for JFrog Artifactory & Xray. All facts link to primary JFrog documentation and reputable sources, current as of June 2026. Product names belong to JFrog Ltd. esc ↑ ↓ navigate ↵ open / or ⌘K to search

title: Access tokens & permissions
body: Skip to content On this page Access tokens Token scopes Creating a token Users, groups, targets Permission targets Set up permissions Home / Access tokens & permissions Platform Security How-to Access tokens & permissions Authentication answers “who are you?”; authorization answers “what may you touch?” JFrog uses access tokens for the first and a Users → Groups → Permission Targets hierarchy for the second. Get this model right and everything else - the REST API, Frogbot, CI - falls into place. Access tokens - the modern way to authenticate Concept Access tokens - the modern way to authenticate Authentication vs. authorization Two words that sound alike but mean different things. Authentication = proving who you are (done with an access token). Authorization = deciding what you're allowed to do (done with the permissions model later on this page). A token gets you in the door; permissions decide which rooms you can enter. An access token is a signed credential that identifies you (or a service) to the JFrog Platform. Unlike a password, a token is scoped (it can be limited to specific resources - "scope" = the set of things it's allowed to touch), revocable (you can kill it without changing your password), and expirable (it can auto-die after a set time). That makes tokens the right choice for CI jobs, the REST API, Frogbot, and IDE plugins. JFrog - Access Tokens . No password exposure. A leaked token can be revoked in isolation; your account password is never embedded in scripts. Scoped to least privilege. Grant a token only the access a given job actually needs. Sent as a Bearer header. Every authenticated REST call uses Authorization: Bearer <token> (see the REST API page). Token scopes: user, admin, and custom Scopes Token scopes: user, admin, and custom Scope What it grants Use for User The same permissions the named user already has (inherits their group memberships and permission targets). A token that acts “as me” for everyday automation. Admin Full administrative access to the platform. Platform bootstrap/IaC only - guard it carefully. Custom / scoped Exactly the resource scopes you specify (e.g. read on one repo). Least-privilege service tokens for a single job. Default to least privilege "Least privilege" means giving each token only the access it truly needs and nothing more. Reach for a custom-scoped token tied to a service identity before an admin token. If a custom token leaks, the "blast radius" (how much damage an attacker could do) is one repo, not your whole platform. Creating an access token - three ways How-to Creating an access token - three ways 1. From the UI Open token management. Administration → User Management → Access Tokens → Generate Token . Choose the scope. Pick the user/identity, set an expiry, and (optionally) restrict the scope. Copy it once. The token value is shown a single time - store it in a secret manager immediately. 2. With the JFrog CLI Create a token (alias jf atc ) jf access-token-create --expiry=3600 # expiry is in seconds: 3600 = 1 hour # scope it to a specific user, or add --groups / --scope jf atc --groups=ci-bots --expiry=86400 # atc = alias; 86400s = 24 hours; token inherits the ci-bots group's access 3. Over the REST API POST to the tokens endpoint # POST = create. Minting a token needs an admin token in the header. # subject: who the token represents | scope: which permissions it gets | expires_in: seconds curl -XPOST -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/access/api/v1/tokens \ -d '{ "subject": "ci-bot", "scope": "applied-permissions/groups:ci-bots", "expires_in": 86400 }' Sources: JFrog - Access Tokens , JFrog CLI - Access Tokens . The authorization model: Users → Groups → Permission Targets Model The authorization model: Users → Groups → Permission Targets JFrog never grants permissions to a user directly on a repo. Instead, permissions flow through a three-layer hierarchy. Understanding it is the key to keeping access manageable as your platform grows. JFrog - Permissions . 1 Users people & service identities → belong to → 2 Groups roles, e.g. ci-bots, devs → assigned in → 3 Permission targets access to repos/builds Assign users to groups (by role), then grant groups access in permission targets . New hires just join a group and inherit the right access - no per-user wiring. JFrog - Permissions . Permission targets - fine-grained access Authorization Permission targets - fine-grained access A permission target binds a set of resources (repositories, builds, or release bundles) to the actions a user/group may perform on them. Actions include read, write/deploy, delete, manage, annotate (set properties), and cache management. You can target specific repos or use wildcards. Element Meaning Resource scope Which repos/builds/release-bundles this target covers. ANY Wildcard - every repository. ANY LOCAL Every local repository. ANY REMOTE Every remote repository. Actions read / deploy(write) / delete / manage / annotate / cache, granted per user or group. Read vs. deploy vs. manage Read = pull/resolve artifacts. Deploy = push new artifacts. Delete = remove them. Manage = edit the permission target itself (grant access to others) - the most powerful, hand it out sparingly. Sources: JFrog - Set up users, groups & permissions , JFrog - Permissions . Wiring up a least-privilege setup How-to Wiring up a least-privilege setup Create role groups. Administration → User Management → Groups → add e.g. developers , ci-bots , release-managers . Add users to groups. Put each person/service identity in the group(s) matching their role. Create permission targets. Administration → User Management → Permissions → add a target; pick the repos (or ANY LOCAL ) and grant each group only the actions it needs. Mint scoped tokens. For automation, create custom-scoped tokens bound to the ci-bots group rather than handing out personal tokens. Review & rotate. Periodically audit targets, expire stale tokens, and confirm no group has more than it needs. Tie it together This model powers everything else on this site: the token you mint here is the Bearer in the REST API page, the JF_ACCESS_TOKEN for Frogbot , and the credential a Pipelines integration stores. ← Previous JFrog Pipelines Next → Kubernetes / Helm setup All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Wiring up a least-privilege setup
body: How-to Wiring up a least-privilege setup Create role groups. Administration → User Management → Groups → add e.g. developers , ci-bots , release-managers . Add users to groups. Put each person/service identity in the group(s) matching their role. Create permission targets. Administration → User Management → Permissions → add a target; pick the repos (or ANY LOCAL ) and grant each group only the actions it needs. Mint scoped tokens. For automation, create custom-scoped tokens bound to the ci-bots group rather than handing out personal tokens. Review & rotate. Periodically audit targets, expire stale tokens, and confirm no group has more than it needs. title: Build promotion in practice
body: Skip to content On this page What promotion is build-info & identity Copy vs. move Promote via CLI Promote via REST Repo layout pattern Home / Build promotion Artifactory Lifecycle CLI + REST Build promotion in practice Promotion is how a binary graduates from dev to staging to release without ever being rebuilt - so what you tested is byte-for-byte what ships. It hinges on one idea: build-info that tracks artifacts by checksum, wherever they move. What build promotion actually is Concept What build promotion actually is First, two words you'll see a lot. A build is one run of your CI/CD pipeline that produces artifacts ("CI/CD" = Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery, the automated build-test-ship flow). A stage is a maturity level a build has reached, such as dev, staging, or released. Build promotion changes a build's status to a more mature stage - for example dev → staging → released - and can optionally move or copy the build's artifacts (and their dependencies) into a target repository and set properties on them ("properties" are key/value tags like stage=released ). The crucial part: no rebuild happens . The same binary that passed your tests is the one that advances. JFrog - How build promotion works . 🎟️ One-sentence analogy Promotion is a stamp on a passport , not a new passport. The traveller (your binary) is unchanged; it just earns a visa for the next country (stage). Because it's the same passport, you have proof the thing entering production is exactly the thing you vetted. build-info and artifact identity Foundation build-info and artifact identity Promotion only works because Artifactory records build-info - a metadata record (a JSON document) capturing which artifacts a build produced, which dependencies it consumed, and the environment it ran in. Think of it as the build's birth certificate. Artifacts are tracked by their checksum - a short fingerprint computed from the file's exact bytes (using the SHA-1 and SHA-256 hashing algorithms); if even one byte changes, the checksum changes, so it uniquely identifies that exact file. Combined with the build.name and build.number properties, Artifactory always knows an artifact's identity no matter what repository it currently sits in. JFrog - Manage builds , Build integration . title: What build promotion actually is
body: Concept What build promotion actually is First, two words you'll see a lot. A build is one run of your CI/CD pipeline that produces artifacts ("CI/CD" = Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery, the automated build-test-ship flow). A stage is a maturity level a build has reached, such as dev, staging, or released. Build promotion changes a build's status to a more mature stage - for example dev → staging → released - and can optionally move or copy the build's artifacts (and their dependencies) into a target repository and set properties on them ("properties" are key/value tags like stage=released ). The crucial part: no rebuild happens . The same binary that passed your tests is the one that advances. JFrog - How build promotion works . 🎟️ One-sentence analogy Promotion is a stamp on a passport , not a new passport. The traveller (your binary) is unchanged; it just earns a visa for the next country (stage). Because it's the same passport, you have proof the thing entering production is exactly the thing you vetted.

title: Frogbot & IDE integration
body: Skip to content On this page What Frogbot is PR scan vs. repo scan Setup on GitHub Actions The workflow YAML IDE integration Home / Frogbot & IDE Xray Shift-left How-to Frogbot & IDE integration Shift security as far left as it goes: Frogbot scans every pull request with Xray and can open fix PRs on its own, while the JFrog IDE plugins flag vulnerable dependencies while you're still typing. What Frogbot is Concept What Frogbot is Two terms first: PR and "shift-left" A pull request (PR) is how you propose changes on GitHub/GitLab - you push your branch and open a PR asking to merge it, so teammates (and bots) can review it before it lands. "Shift-left" means catching problems as early as possible in the development timeline (drawn left-to-right), because the earlier you catch a bug or vulnerability, the cheaper it is to fix. Frogbot is a Git bot that scans your pull requests and repositories for security vulnerabilities using JFrog Xray (Xray is JFrog's security scanner). When a developer opens a PR, Frogbot scans it and posts the results as a comment (JFrog calls this a "decoration") directly on the PR - and if it finds no new issues, it says so. It works with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure Repos. jfrog/frogbot . Only flags new issues. A PR scan compares the project state before and after the change, so it reports only vulnerabilities the PR introduces - not pre-existing noise. Opens fix PRs. When fixable issues are found and auto-fix is enabled, Frogbot opens a pull request that upgrades the vulnerable dependency to a fixed version. Requires Xray 3.29.0+. Frogbot uses your JFrog platform's Xray to do the actual scanning. JFrog - Commit & PR scan . Pull-request scan vs. repository scan Two modes Pull-request scan vs. repository scan Mode Command What it does Pull Request frogbot scan-pull-request Scans a PR by diffing before/after the change. Only new issues are reported, as PR comments. Repository (commit) scan frogbot scan-repository Scans the latest commit of a branch for all existing vulnerabilities. With auto-fix on, opens fix PRs for upgradable dependencies. Source: JFrog - Scanning Commits and Pull Requests . Required environment variables An "environment variable" is a named value the running job can read. Both modes need JF_URL (your platform URL, e.g. https://my.jfrog.io ) and JF_ACCESS_TOKEN (a scoped access token - see the Access tokens page), plus a Git token ( JF_GIT_TOKEN / the CI's built-in token) so Frogbot has permission to comment and open PRs on your repo. Setting up Frogbot on GitHub Actions How-to Setting up Frogbot on GitHub Actions Allow Frogbot to open PRs. In GitHub → Settings → Actions → General , check "Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests." (Recommended for OSS) Create a frogbot environment. Settings → Environments → New environment named frogbot , and add reviewers. Those reviewers must approve before Frogbot scans a PR - a safety gate for public repos. Add the workflow file. Drop a Frogbot workflow into .github/workflows/ (template below), or use the JFrog GitHub App to have it opened for you automatically across selected repos. Add secrets/vars. Provide JF_URL and JF_ACCESS_TOKEN as repository secrets. The built-in GITHUB_TOKEN covers Git operations. Sources: JFrog - Frogbot GitHub Actions install , JFrog GitHub App bulk install . The PR-scan workflow YAML The PR-scan workflow A minimal GitHub Actions workflow that runs Frogbot on every pull request. A "workflow" is a YAML file in .github/workflows/ that GitHub runs automatically when a trigger fires. YAML is an indentation-based config format - indentation (spaces, never tabs) defines the nesting. JFrog - GitHub PR scans . name: "Frogbot Scan Pull Request" # label shown in the Actions tab on: # what triggers this workflow pull_request_target: # run when a PR is opened/updated types: [ opened, synchronize ] # "synchronize" = new commits pushed to the PR permissions: # what the job is allowed to do pull-requests: write # needed to post the scan comment contents: read # read the repo files to scan them id-token: write jobs: scan-pull-request: runs-on: ubuntu-latest # the VM type GitHub runs the job on # PR must be approved by a "frogbot" environment reviewer before scanning environment: frogbot steps: - uses: jfrog/frogbot@v2 # the official Frogbot action, version 2 env: # env vars passed to Frogbot JF_URL: ${{ vars.JF_URL }} # vars.* = a repo variable (not secret) JF_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JF_ACCESS_TOKEN }} # secrets.* = an encrypted repo secret JF_GIT_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} # auto-provided by GitHub Actions For the repository (auto-fix) mode, use a separate workflow triggered on push to your default branch (or on a schedule) that runs Frogbot in scan-repository mode. Configure Frogbot in GitHub Actions . IDE integration - catch it before the PR Shift-left IDE integration - catch it before the PR Even earlier than the PR is the editor. JFrog ships plugins for VS Code, IntelliJ/JetBrains IDEs, Visual Studio, and Eclipse that scan your project's dependencies against Xray and surface vulnerabilities inline as you work. JFrog - IDE integrations . 🧩 Inline findings Vulnerable dependencies are highlighted in your dependency files with severity and details, without leaving the editor. 🔗 Connect to your JPD Point the plugin at your JFrog platform URL + an access token; it uses your Xray for the database and policies. ⏪ Earliest feedback Fix issues before you even commit - the cheapest place to fix anything is in the editor. The shift-left ladder IDE plugin (as you type) → Frogbot (on the PR) → Xray indexing + Watch (in Artifactory) → build-scan gate (in CI). Each rung catches issues later and more expensively, so push detection as far left as you can. The Watch and the policies behind every rung are covered in Xray Policies & Watches . ← Previous Artifactory REST API Next → JFrog Pipelines All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: What Frogbot is
body: Concept What Frogbot is Two terms first: PR and "shift-left" A pull request (PR) is how you propose changes on GitHub/GitLab - you push your branch and open a PR asking to merge it, so teammates (and bots) can review it before it lands. "Shift-left" means catching problems as early as possible in the development timeline (drawn left-to-right), because the earlier you catch a bug or vulnerability, the cheaper it is to fix. Frogbot is a Git bot that scans your pull requests and repositories for security vulnerabilities using JFrog Xray (Xray is JFrog's security scanner). When a developer opens a PR, Frogbot scans it and posts the results as a comment (JFrog calls this a "decoration") directly on the PR - and if it finds no new issues, it says so. It works with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure Repos. jfrog/frogbot . Only flags new issues. A PR scan compares the project state before and after the change, so it reports only vulnerabilities the PR introduces - not pre-existing noise. Opens fix PRs. When fixable issues are found and auto-fix is enabled, Frogbot opens a pull request that upgrades the vulnerable dependency to a fixed version. Requires Xray 3.29.0+. Frogbot uses your JFrog platform's Xray to do the actual scanning. JFrog - Commit & PR scan .

title: Pull-request scan vs. repository scan
body: Two modes Pull-request scan vs. repository scan Mode Command What it does Pull Request frogbot scan-pull-request Scans a PR by diffing before/after the change. Only new issues are reported, as PR comments. Repository (commit) scan frogbot scan-repository Scans the latest commit of a branch for all existing vulnerabilities. With auto-fix on, opens fix PRs for upgradable dependencies. Source: JFrog - Scanning Commits and Pull Requests . Required environment variables An "environment variable" is a named value the running job can read. Both modes need JF_URL (your platform URL, e.g. https://my.jfrog.io ) and JF_ACCESS_TOKEN (a scoped access token - see the Access tokens page), plus a Git token ( JF_GIT_TOKEN / the CI's built-in token) so Frogbot has permission to comment and open PRs on your repo.

title: Setting up Frogbot on GitHub Actions
body: How-to Setting up Frogbot on GitHub Actions Allow Frogbot to open PRs. In GitHub → Settings → Actions → General , check "Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests." (Recommended for OSS) Create a frogbot environment. Settings → Environments → New environment named frogbot , and add reviewers. Those reviewers must approve before Frogbot scans a PR - a safety gate for public repos. Add the workflow file. Drop a Frogbot workflow into .github/workflows/ (template below), or use the JFrog GitHub App to have it opened for you automatically across selected repos. Add secrets/vars. Provide JF_URL and JF_ACCESS_TOKEN as repository secrets. The built-in GITHUB_TOKEN covers Git operations. Sources: JFrog - Frogbot GitHub Actions install , JFrog GitHub App bulk install .

title: The PR-scan workflow
body: YAML The PR-scan workflow A minimal GitHub Actions workflow that runs Frogbot on every pull request. A "workflow" is a YAML file in .github/workflows/ that GitHub runs automatically when a trigger fires. YAML is an indentation-based config format - indentation (spaces, never tabs) defines the nesting. JFrog - GitHub PR scans . name: "Frogbot Scan Pull Request" # label shown in the Actions tab on: # what triggers this workflow pull_request_target: # run when a PR is opened/updated types: [ opened, synchronize ] # "synchronize" = new commits pushed to the PR permissions: # what the job is allowed to do pull-requests: write # needed to post the scan comment contents: read # read the repo files to scan them id-token: write jobs: scan-pull-request: runs-on: ubuntu-latest # the VM type GitHub runs the job on # PR must be approved by a "frogbot" environment reviewer before scanning environment: frogbot steps: - uses: jfrog/frogbot@v2 # the official Frogbot action, version 2 env: # env vars passed to Frogbot JF_URL: ${{ vars.JF_URL }} # vars.* = a repo variable (not secret) JF_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JF_ACCESS_TOKEN }} # secrets.* = an encrypted repo secret JF_GIT_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} # auto-provided by GitHub Actions For the repository (auto-fix) mode, use a separate workflow triggered on push to your default branch (or on a schedule) that runs Frogbot in scan-repository mode. title: Artifactory & Xray from first principles
body: Skip to content On this page The problem it solves Vocabulary first What Artifactory is The 4 repository types How it fits together Package types What Xray is How scanning works Policies & Watches Choose your track CLI cheat sheet Full mental model Home / Fundamentals Artifactory Xray Beginner → core Artifactory & Xray from first principles Two products, one job: control every binary your software is built from, and prove it's safe. This page builds the whole picture from the bottom up - plain-language concepts, diagrams, comparison tables, then real hands-on labs. The problem these tools solve Foundations The problem these tools solve Before any product names, understand the pain. When you build software, your code is only a small part of what actually ships. The rest is binaries - compiled libraries, container images, packages - that you either build yourself or download from the internet. What is a "binary" / "artifact"? Anything your build produces or consumes that isn't source code: a .jar , an npm package, a Docker image, a Python wheel, a Helm chart, a compiled .exe . The generic industry word is artifact . JFrog calls itself an "artifact repository manager" - a place to store and serve these. Without a central system, teams hit four recurring problems: 01 Where do builds get dependencies? Everyone pulls directly from the public internet (npm registry, Docker Hub, Maven Central). It's slow, breaks when those sites are down, and you have no control over what enters your builds. 02 Where do our builds go? You compile something internally. Where does it live so other teams and your CI/CD can reliably grab the exact version? Email and shared drives don't scale. 03 Is any of it dangerous? A public package might carry a known vulnerability (a CVE ) or a banned open-source license. You need to know before it reaches production - not after a breach. 04 Can we prove what's inside? Auditors and customers increasingly ask: "List every component in this release." That list is called an SBOM . Producing it by hand is impossible. Artifactory solves problems 1, 2 & 4 - it's the single, trusted home for all binaries flowing in and out. Xray solves problem 3 (and helps with 4) - it continuously scans everything Artifactory stores for vulnerabilities and license risks. They are designed to work together: Artifactory is the warehouse, Xray is the security inspector standing inside it. JFrog - What is Artifactory . Vocabulary you'll see everywhere Foundations Vocabulary you'll see everywhere JFrog docs assume you know these. Skim now, refer back later. Artifact / Binary Any built/downloaded file: a package, image, library. The thing being stored. Repository A named container inside Artifactory that holds artifacts of one package type (e.g. a Docker repo, an npm repo). Package type The ecosystem format - Docker, Maven, npm, PyPI, Helm, etc. Artifactory "speaks" each one natively. Registry The package-ecosystem word for a repository (e.g. "Docker registry"). Same idea. Dependency A third-party artifact your code needs to build or run. CVE Common Vulnerabilities & Exposures - a globally unique ID for a known security flaw, e.g. CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell). SCA Software Composition Analysis - scanning the open-source pieces of your software for risk. This is Xray's core job. SBOM Software Bill of Materials - a complete, machine-readable inventory of every component in a release (formats: SPDX, CycloneDX). JPD JFrog Platform Deployment - one running instance of the JFrog platform (your server / cloud tenant). build-info Metadata Artifactory records about a build: which artifacts, which dependencies, which environment. Enables traceability. Promotion Moving an artifact from one repo to the next as it passes quality gates (dev → staging → release). Watch + Policy Xray's enforcement pair: a Policy defines the rules, a Watch says where to apply them. (Detailed later.) What Artifactory actually is Artifactory What Artifactory actually is JFrog Artifactory is a universal binary repository manager - one central place to store, version, secure and serve every artifact your organization produces or depends on. "Universal" means it natively supports 40+ package formats (Docker, Maven, npm, PyPI, Helm, NuGet, Go, Terraform, even ML models) at the same time. JFrog Artifactory . 📦 One-sentence analogy Artifactory is a smart warehouse for software parts . Parts arrive from outside suppliers (the public internet), parts are manufactured in-house (your builds), and everything is catalogued, version-stamped, access-controlled, and handed out through a single front desk. Three things make it more than a file server: It speaks every package language. Your npm install , docker pull , or mvn build point at Artifactory and it behaves exactly like the native registry would. It proxies and caches the internet. Public dependencies pass through Artifactory once, get cached, and stay available even if the upstream source goes down. Stack Overflow explanation . It's a control point. Because everything flows through it, you can apply access control, scanning (Xray), and policies in one place. JFrog overview . The 4 repository types (the core concept) Artifactory The 4 repository types (the core concept) If you learn only one thing about Artifactory, learn this. Every repository is one of four types. They combine into a clean pattern that every JFrog setup follows. Outside world Public registries Docker Hub · npm · Maven Central · PyPI proxy & cache → Inside Artifactory REMOTE repo caches external artifacts LOCAL repo stores your own builds ▸ both wrapped by ▸ VIRTUAL repo one URL = local + remote combined single URL → Developer / CI your build tool npm · docker · mvn · pip The standard pattern: developers and CI talk to one virtual repo URL ; Artifactory routes deploys to the local repo and resolves dependencies from local first, then the remote cache, then the internet. Federated repos (4th type) sit alongside for multi-site sync. JFrog Repository Management . Type What it does Can you upload to it? Real-world use Local Physically stores artifacts your org creates internally. Yes - this is your deploy target. Your CI pushes built Docker images, release JARs, internal libraries here. Remote A caching proxy for an external registry. Fetches on demand, caches locally. No - artifacts arrive by proxying. A proxy of Docker Hub or npm registry so builds are fast and survive outages. Virtual Aggregates many local + remote repos under one URL . Resolves in priority order. Via default deploy target (points to a local). The single endpoint your developers configure. Hides internal/external complexity. Federated A local repo auto-mirrored bi-directionally across multiple sites (JPDs). Yes - like a local, but synced. Global teams (London ↔ Tokyo) sharing one source of truth. Enterprise tier. Sources: JFrog config entities , JFrog Federated Repositories . Local/remote/virtual need a Pro subscription; federated needs Enterprise X+. The "aha" moment A virtual repo is the magic layer. Your npm client points at one URL. Behind it, Artifactory checks your local repo first (internal packages), then the remote cache (previously downloaded public packages), and only then reaches out to the public internet. The developer never has to know or care where a package lives. JFrog artifact management demo . Want to go deeper on keeping repos in sync across sites? See Replication & Federation . How it fits in your pipeline Artifactory How it fits in your pipeline Zoom out. Here's where Artifactory sits in a normal CI/CD flow, and where Xray plugs in. This is the picture to hold in your head. 1 Developer writes code, runs build locally → 2 CI/CD GitHub Actions builds artifact → 3 Artifactory stores artifact + records build-info ↓ every artifact is indexed & scanned 4 Xray scans for CVEs & license risk → 5 Policy gate pass → promote · fail → block/alert → 6 Production only approved artifacts ship Artifacts get promoted through repositories as they pass each gate. Xray watches the whole warehouse continuously, so a CVE disclosed tomorrow still flags an artifact you stored last month. JFrog DevSecOps best practices . What's "promotion"? You typically have separate repos per maturity stage: dev-local → staging-local → release-local . Promotion = moving (or copying) an artifact up a stage once it passes tests and scans. Same binary, no rebuild - so what you tested is exactly what ships. Full walkthrough on the Build promotion page. Package types it supports Artifactory Package types it supports "Universal" is the selling point: 40+ ecosystems, one platform. You don't need them all - pick the ones your stack uses. Here are the common ones a DevOps team touches. Full list . Docker / OCI Helm Maven Gradle npm PyPI Go NuGet Cargo (Rust) Conan (C++) Debian RPM / YUM Terraform Conda Hugging Face (ML) RubyGems Generic (anything) DevOps reality check For a typical cloud-native team, you'll create repos for: Docker (your images), Helm (your charts), and a remote proxy of Docker Hub . Add npm / PyPI / Go remotes per language. The Generic type stores literally anything (tarballs, configs, ML weights) when no native type fits. What Xray actually is Xray What Xray actually is JFrog Xray is a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool. It scans everything Artifactory stores - packages, container images, builds - to detect security vulnerabilities, malicious packages, license risks, and operational issues. It's natively integrated with Artifactory, so binary scanning turns on with a single checkbox. JFrog Xray docs . 🔍 One-sentence analogy If Artifactory is the warehouse, Xray is the security inspector living inside it - X-raying every box that arrives, cross-checking it against a global database of known threats, and raising an alarm (or locking the door) when something dangerous is found. 🛡️ Vulnerabilities Matches components against CVE databases (NVD, GitHub, vendor feeds + JFrog's own security research) to find known flaws. ⚖️ License compliance Flags open-source licenses your org bans or must track (e.g. GPL in a proprietary product). 🐛 Malicious packages Detects deliberately poisoned packages - including malicious ML models on Hugging Face. 📋 SBOM & impact Generates a full Software Bill of Materials and shows the impact graph of any flaw across your components. Sources: Xray architecture overview , JFrog SBOM . How scanning actually works Xray How scanning actually works Xray's superpower is deep recursive scanning . It doesn't just look at your top-level package - it peels open every layer: dependencies, dependencies-of-dependencies, files inside Docker layers, archives inside archives. JFrog Impact Analysis . Deep recursive scan - peeling the onion Your Docker image Base OS layer App package (npm/jar) Direct dependency ↳ vulnerable lib · CVE-2021-44228 Xray finds the vulnerable component buried 5 levels deep, then uses impact analysis to show every artifact, build, and image affected by it. JFrog vulnerability detection . The scanning lifecycle Index. When a repo is marked for Xray, every artifact is broken into its component graph and indexed. Scan. Each component is matched against the vulnerability + license databases. Continuous re-check. When a new CVE is published, Xray re-evaluates already-stored artifacts - no rescan needed. This is why it catches yesterday's "safe" image today. Report & enforce. Findings appear as violations (when they break a policy) with severity, affected components, and remediation (which version fixes it). Understanding scan results . Where you can trigger a scan How Best for Inside Artifactory (automatic) Enable Xray indexing on a repo - scans on upload Continuous coverage of everything stored CI/CD pipeline jf build-scan / jf scan Failing a build before bad code ships Local machine (shift-left) jf scan on source, jf docker scan on an image Catching issues before you even commit IDE / Pull request JFrog IDE plugins & Frogbot Instant developer feedback + autofix PRs Source: JFrog CLI command summaries . For PR scanning and IDE setup, see Frogbot & IDE integration . Policies & Watches (how enforcement works) Xray Policies & Watches (how enforcement works) Finding problems is useless unless you act on them. Xray's enforcement runs on two linked objects. This trips up newcomers, so here's the clean split: Defines the RULES Policy A set of rules describing what counts as a problem and what to do about it. Type: Security · License · Operational risk Rule: e.g. "severity ≥ High" or "license = GPL" Action: notify · fail build · block download · create ticket applied via Defines the SCOPE Watch A binding that says where a policy applies and turns it on. Targets: specific repos, builds, or projects Attaches: one or more policies Result: matches → violations get generated Remember it this way Policy = the law (what's illegal + the punishment). Watch = the jurisdiction + the police (where the law applies and who enforces it). A finding only becomes a violation when a Watch points a Policy at the repo where it lives. JFrog Policy & Governance . Severity levels you'll triage Critical High Medium Low Unknown A common starter policy: "generate violations for High + Critical security issues, but only when a fixed version is available" - so you're not alerted on problems you can't yet fix. Stack Overflow on fix-version rule . Choose your hands-on track Hands-on Choose your hands-on track Reading gets you understanding; doing gets you skill. Pick a track based on how deep you want to go. All three reach the same goal: store an artifact, then scan it with Xray. ☁️ JFrog Cloud (free tier) 🐳 Local Docker (self-hosted) ⌨️ Concepts + CLI Fastest path to a working platform. JFrog runs a free cloud tier (AWS/Azure/GCP) - no install, ready in minutes. Best first stop. Start free . 1 Sign up for the free cloud subscription. Go to jfrog.com/start-free → choose Cloud (free). You get a tenant like yourname.jfrog.io with Artifactory + Xray. Free tier: ~2 GB storage, 10 GB transfer. Free tier announcement . 2 Log in to the web UI. Explore the two main tabs: Application (browse repos/artifacts) and Administration (create repos, configure Xray). 3 Create your first repositories. Administration → Repositories → + Add Repository . Make a Local Docker repo ( docker-local ), a Remote proxy of Docker Hub ( docker-remote ), and a Virtual repo combining both ( docker ). Repo creation walkthrough . 4 Connect the CLI. Install JFrog CLI (see the CLI tab) then run jf c add and paste your *.jfrog.io URL + a token. Verify with jf rt ping . 5 Push an image. jf docker push yourname.jfrog.io/docker-local/demo:1.0 - then find it in the UI under your local repo. 6 Turn on Xray. Administration → Xray → Indexed Resources → add docker-local . The image scans automatically. 7 Create a Policy + Watch. Administration → Xray → Watches & Policies . Make a Security policy (rule: severity ≥ High, action: notify) and a Watch targeting docker-local . Policies/watches video . 8 Read the results. Open the artifact → Xray tab. Review vulnerabilities by severity, affected components, and fix versions. You've completed the loop. 🎉 Why start here Zero infrastructure to maintain, real cloud UI, and everything in this guide works out of the box. Graduate to local Docker once you want to understand the moving parts. Run the whole platform on your Mac (OrbStack/Docker). You'll see exactly what services make up Artifactory + Xray. Requires Docker Engine 25+ & Docker Compose v2; ARM64 (Apple Silicon) is supported via Docker. Install guide . Heads up on resources The full platform (Artifactory + Xray + PostgreSQL + RabbitMQ) wants ~4 CPU / 8 GB RAM. Your 8 GB Mac setup is the realistic minimum - close other heavy apps. 1 Download the trial Docker-Compose bundle. curl -L "https://releases.jfrog.io/artifactory/jfrog-prox/org/artifactory/pro/docker/jfrog-platform-trial-prox/[RELEASE]/jfrog-platform-trial-prox-[RELEASE]-compose.tar.gz" -o jfrog-compose.tar.gz Source 2 Extract & enter the directory. tar -xvf jfrog-compose.tar.gz cd jfrog-platform-trial-prox-* 3 Run the config script (sets up folder structure + the .env file Compose uses). Follow its prompts. 4 Start everything. docker compose -p trial-pro up -d Give it a few minutes - Xray boots after Artifactory and PostgreSQL are healthy. Xray install steps . 5 Open the UI. Browse to http://localhost:8082 . Default login admin / password - change it immediately. 6 Apply your free license. Paste the license key from your JFrog account to unlock Xray. 7 From here, follow Cloud steps 3-8 - create local/remote/virtual repos, push an image, enable Xray indexing, set a policy + watch, read results. The UI is identical. 8 Tear down cleanly. docker compose -p trial-pro down Add -v only if you want to wipe the data volumes too. Prefer Kubernetes? See Kubernetes / Helm setup for installing Artifactory on a cluster with the JFrog Helm charts. For when you don't want to run a server - just learn the commands & integration patterns. The JFrog CLI binary is jf . One tool drives Artifactory and Xray. CLI summaries . 1 Install the CLI (macOS, Homebrew): brew install jfrog-cli Or one-liner: curl -fL https://install-cli.jfrog.io | sh . Verify: jf --version . 2 Configure a server connection. jf c add my-jpd --url=https://yourname.jfrog.io --interactive Use an access token, not a password. Test: jf rt ping . 3 Scan your source code (shift-left) - no upload needed: jf scan . Scans the dependencies in the current project against Xray. Source-scan docs . 4 Scan a local Docker image: jf docker scan my-app:1.0.0 First run downloads ~100 MB of Xray scanner binaries. jf docker docs . 5 Scan an artifact already in Artifactory, against a watch: jf xr scan --target "docker-local/demo:1.0" --watches "my-watch" Xray CLI commands . 6 Wire it into GitHub Actions (fail the build on Critical issues): - name: Security Scan run: jf build-scan ${{ github.repository }} ${{ github.run_number }} --fail=true --vuln=Critical CI integration . Note: the build must be added under Administration → Xray → Indexed Resources first. Mental model for the CLI jf rt … = Artifactory (uploads, downloads, repos). jf xr … = Xray (scans). jf scan / jf docker scan / jf build-scan = convenient shortcuts that combine both. CLI cheat sheet Hands-on CLI cheat sheet The commands you'll reach for most. jf is the binary; older docs use jfrog . Reference . matches components against cve databases (nvd, github, vendor feeds + jfrog's own security research) to find known flaws. ⚖️ license compliance flags open-source licenses your org bans or must track materials and shows the impact graph of any flaw across your components. sources: xray architecture overview , jfrog sbom . how scanning actually works xray how scanning actually works xray's superpower is deep recursive scanning . it doesn't just look at your top-level package - it peels open every layer: dependencies, dependencies-of-dependencies, files inside docker layers, archives the vulnerable component buried 5 levels deep, then uses impact analysis to show every artifact, build, and image affected by it. jfrog vulnerability detection . the scanning lifecycle index. when a repo is marked for xray, every artifact is broken into its component graph and indexed. scan. each component is matched against the vulnerability + license databases. continuous re-check. when a new cve is published, xray re-evaluates already-stored artifacts - no rescan needed. this is why it catches yesterday's "safe" image today. report & enforce. findings appear as violations (when they break a policy) with severity, affected components, and remediation (which version fixes it). understanding scan results . where you can trigger a scan how best for inside artifactory (automatic) enable xray indexing on a repo - scans on upload continuous coverage of everything stored ci/cd pipeline jf build-scan / jf scan failing a build before bad code ships local machine (shift-left) jf scan on them. xray's enforcement runs on two linked objects. this trips up newcomers, so here's the clean split: defines the rules policy a set of rules describing what counts as a problem and what to do about it. type: security · license · operational risk rule: e.g. "severity ≥ high" or "license = gpl" action: notify · fail build · block download · create ticket applied via defines the scope watch a binding that says where a policy applies and turns it on. targets: specific repos, builds, or projects attaches: one or more policies result: matches → violations get generated remember it this way policy = the law (what's illegal + the punishment). watch = the jurisdiction + the police (where the law applies and who enforces it). a finding only becomes a violation when a watch points a policy at the repo where it lives. jfrog policy & governance . severity levels you'll triage critical high medium low unknown a common starter policy: "generate violations for high + critical security issues, but only when a fixed version is available" - so you're not alerted on problems you can't yet fix. stack overflow on fix-version rule . choose your hands-on track hands-on choose your hands-on ). repo creation walkthrough . 4 connect the cli. install jfrog cli (see the cli tab) then run jf c add and paste your *.jfrog.io url + a token. verify with jf rt ping . 5 push an image. jf docker policies/watches video . 8 read the results. open the artifact → xray tab. review vulnerabilities by severity, affected components, and fix versions. you've completed the loop. 🎉 why start here zero infrastructure to maintain, real cloud ui, and everything in this guide works out of the box. graduate to local docker once you want to understand the moving parts. run the whole platform on your mac on resources the full platform (artifactory + xray + postgresql + rabbitmq) wants ~4 cpu / 8 gb ram. your 8 gb mac setup is the realistic minimum - close other heavy apps. 1 download the trial jfrog-compose.tar.gz source 2 extract & enter the directory. tar -xvf jfrog-compose.tar.gz cd jfrog-platform-trial-prox-* 3 run the config script (sets up folder structure + the .env file compose uses). follow its prompts. 4 start everything. docker compose -p trial-pro up -d give it a few minutes - xray boots after artifactory and postgresql are healthy. xray install steps . 5 open the ui. browse to http://localhost:8082 . default login admin / password - change it immediately. 6 apply your free license. paste the license key from your jfrog account to unlock xray. 7 from here, follow title: The problem these tools solve
body: Foundations The problem these tools solve Before any product names, understand the pain. When you build software, your code is only a small part of what actually ships. The rest is binaries - compiled libraries, container images, packages - that you either build yourself or download from the internet. What is a "binary" / "artifact"? Anything your build produces or consumes that isn't source code: a .jar , an npm package, a Docker image, a Python wheel, a Helm chart, a compiled .exe . The generic industry word is artifact . JFrog calls itself an "artifact repository manager" - a place to store and serve these. Without a central system, teams hit four recurring problems: 01 Where do builds get dependencies? Everyone pulls directly from the public internet (npm registry, Docker Hub, Maven Central). It's slow, breaks when those sites are down, and you have no control over what enters your builds. 02 Where do our builds go? You compile something internally. Where does it live so other teams and your CI/CD can reliably grab the exact version? Email and shared drives don't scale. 03 Is any of it dangerous? A public package might carry a known vulnerability (a CVE ) or a banned open-source license. You need to know before it reaches production - not after a breach. 04 Can we prove what's inside? Auditors and customers increasingly ask: "List every component in this release." That list is called an SBOM . Producing it by hand is impossible. Artifactory solves problems 1, 2 & 4 - it's the single, trusted home for all binaries flowing in and out. Xray solves problem 3 (and helps with 4) - it continuously scans everything Artifactory stores for vulnerabilities and license risks. They are designed to work together: Artifactory is the warehouse, Xray is the security inspector standing inside it. JFrog - What is Artifactory .

JFrog Artifactory . 📦 One-sentence analogy Artifactory is a smart warehouse for software parts . title: The 4 repository types (the core concept)
body: Artifactory The 4 repository types (the core concept) If you learn only one thing about Artifactory, learn this. Every repository is one of four types. They combine into a clean pattern that every JFrog setup follows. Outside world Public registries Docker Hub · npm · Maven Central · PyPI proxy & cache → Inside Artifactory REMOTE repo caches external artifacts LOCAL repo stores your own builds ▸ both wrapped by ▸ VIRTUAL repo one URL = local + remote combined single URL → Developer / CI your build tool npm · docker · mvn · pip The standard pattern: developers and CI talk to one virtual repo URL ; Artifactory routes deploys to the local repo and resolves dependencies from local first, then the remote cache, then the internet. Federated repos (4th type) sit alongside for multi-site sync. JFrog Repository Management . Type What it does Can you upload to it? Real-world use Local Physically stores artifacts your org creates internally. Yes - this is your deploy target. Your CI pushes built Docker images, release JARs, internal libraries here. Remote A caching proxy for an external registry. Fetches on demand, caches locally. No - artifacts arrive by proxying. A proxy of Docker Hub or npm registry so builds are fast and survive outages. Virtual Aggregates many local + remote repos under one URL . Resolves in priority order. Via default deploy target (points to a local). The single endpoint your developers configure. Hides internal/external complexity. Federated A local repo auto-mirrored bi-directionally across multiple sites (JPDs). Yes - like a local, but synced. Global teams (London ↔ Tokyo) sharing one source of truth. Enterprise tier. Sources: JFrog config entities , JFrog Federated Repositories . Local/remote/virtual need a Pro subscription; federated needs Enterprise X+. The "aha" moment A virtual repo is the magic layer. Your npm client points at one URL. Behind it, Artifactory checks your local repo first (internal packages), then the remote cache (previously downloaded public packages), and only then reaches out to the public internet. _body: artifactory the 4 repository types (the core concept) if you learn only one thing about artifactory, learn this. every repository is one of four types. they combine into a clean pattern that every jfrog setup follows. outside world public registries docker hub · npm · maven central · pypi proxy & cache → inside artifactory remote repo caches external artifacts local repo stores your own builds ▸ both wrapped by ▸ virtual repo one url = local + remote combined single url → developer / ci your build tool npm · docker · mvn · pip the standard pattern: developers and ci talk to one virtual repo url ; artifactory routes deploys to the local repo and resolves dependencies from local first, then the remote cache, then the internet. federated repos (4th type) sit alongside for multi-site sync. jfrog repository management . type what it does can you upload to it? real-world use local physically stores artifacts your org creates internally. yes - this is your deploy target. your ci pushes built docker images, release jars, internal libraries here. remote a caching proxy for an external registry. fetches on demand, caches locally. no - artifacts arrive by proxying. a proxy of docker hub or npm registry so builds are fast and survive outages. virtual aggregates many local + remote repos under one url . resolves in priority order. via default deploy target (points to a local). the single endpoint your developers configure. hides internal/external complexity. federated a local repo auto-mirrored bi-directionally across multiple sites (jpds). yes - like a local, but synced. global teams (london ↔ tokyo) sharing one source of truth. enterprise tier. sources: jfrog config entities , jfrog federated repositories . local/remote/virtual need a pro subscription; federated needs enterprise x+. the "aha" moment a virtual repo is the magic layer. your npm client points at one url. behind it, artifactory checks your local repo first (internal packages), then the remote cache (previously downloaded public packages), and only then reaches out to the public internet. the developer never has to know or care where a package lives. jfrog artifact management demo . want to go deeper on keeping repos in sync across sites? see replication & federation .

title: How it fits in your pipeline
body: Artifactory How it fits in your pipeline Zoom out. Here's where Artifactory sits in a normal CI/CD flow, and where Xray plugs in. This is the picture to hold in your head. 1 Developer writes code, runs build locally → 2 CI/CD GitHub Actions builds artifact → 3 Artifactory stores artifact + records build-info ↓ every artifact is indexed & scanned 4 Xray scans for CVEs & license risk → 5 Policy gate pass → promote · fail → block/alert → 6 Production only approved artifacts ship Artifacts get promoted through repositories as they pass each gate. Xray watches the whole warehouse continuously, so a CVE disclosed tomorrow still flags an artifact you stored last month. JFrog DevSecOps best practices . What's "promotion"? You typically have separate repos per maturity stage: dev-local → staging-local → release-local . Promotion = moving (or copying) an artifact up a stage once it passes tests and scans. Same binary, no rebuild - so what you tested is exactly what ships. Full walkthrough on the Build promotion page.

title: What Xray actually is
body: Xray What Xray actually is JFrog Xray is a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool. It scans everything Artifactory stores - packages, container images, builds - to detect security vulnerabilities, malicious packages, license risks, and operational issues. It's natively integrated with Artifactory, so binary scanning turns on with a single checkbox. JFrog Xray docs . 🔍 One-sentence analogy If Artifactory is the warehouse, Xray is the security inspector living inside it - X-raying every box that arrives, cross-checking it against a global database of known threats, and raising an alarm (or locking the door) when something dangerous is found. 🛡️ Vulnerabilities Matches components against CVE databases (NVD, GitHub, vendor feeds + JFrog's own security research) to find known flaws. ⚖️ License compliance Flags open-source licenses your org bans or must track (e.g. GPL in a proprietary product). 🐛 Malicious packages Detects deliberately poisoned packages - including malicious ML models on Hugging Face. 📋 SBOM & impact Generates a full Software Bill of Materials and shows the impact graph of any flaw across your components. Sources: Xray architecture overview , JFrog SBOM .

title: How scanning actually works
body: Xray How scanning actually works Xray's superpower is deep recursive scanning . It doesn't just look at your top-level package - it peels open every layer: dependencies, dependencies-of-dependencies, files inside Docker layers, archives inside archives. JFrog Impact Analysis . Deep recursive scan - peeling the onion Your Docker image Base OS layer App package (npm/jar) Direct dependency ↳ vulnerable lib · CVE-2021-44228 Xray finds the vulnerable component buried 5 levels deep, then uses impact analysis to show every artifact, build, and image affected by it. JFrog vulnerability detection . The scanning lifecycle Index. When a repo is marked for Xray, every artifact is broken into its component graph and indexed. Scan. Each component is matched against the vulnerability + license databases. Continuous re-check. When a new CVE is published, Xray re-evaluates already-stored artifacts - no rescan needed. This is why it catches yesterday's "safe" image today. Report & enforce. Findings appear as violations (when they break a policy) with severity, affected components, and remediation (which version fixes it). Understanding scan results . Where you can trigger a scan How Best for Inside Artifactory (automatic) Enable Xray indexing on a repo - scans on upload Continuous coverage of everything stored CI/CD pipeline jf build-scan / jf scan Failing a build before bad code ships Local machine (shift-left) jf scan on source, jf docker scan on an image Catching issues before you even commit IDE / Pull request JFrog IDE plugins & Frogbot Instant developer feedback + autofix PRs Source: JFrog CLI command summaries . For PR scanning and IDE setup, see Frogbot & IDE integration .

title: Policies & Watches (how enforcement works)
body: Xray Policies & Watches (how enforcement works) Finding problems is useless unless you act on them. Xray's enforcement runs on two linked objects. This trips up newcomers, so here's the clean split: Defines the RULES Policy A set of rules describing what counts as a problem and what to do about it. Type: Security · License · Operational risk Rule: e.g. "severity ≥ High" or "license = GPL" Action: notify · fail build · block download · create ticket applied via Defines the SCOPE Watch A binding that says where a policy applies and turns it on. Targets: specific repos, builds, or projects Attaches: one or more policies Result: matches → violations get generated Remember it this way Policy = the law (what's illegal + the punishment). Watch = the jurisdiction + the police (where the law applies and who enforces it). A finding only becomes a violation when a Watch points a Policy at the repo where it lives. JFrog Policy & Governance . Severity levels you'll triage Critical High Medium Low Unknown A common starter policy: "generate violations for High + Critical security issues, but only when a fixed version is available" - so you're not alerted on problems you can't yet fix. Stack Overflow on fix-version rule .

title: Choose your hands-on track
body: Hands-on Choose your hands-on track Reading gets you understanding; doing gets you skill. Pick a track based on how deep you want to go. All three reach the same goal: store an artifact, then scan it with Xray. ☁️ JFrog Cloud (free tier) 🐳 Local Docker (self-hosted) ⌨️ Concepts + CLI Fastest path to a working platform. JFrog runs a free cloud tier (AWS/Azure/GCP) - no install, ready in minutes. Best first stop. Start free . 1 Sign up for the free cloud subscription. Go to jfrog.com/start-free → choose Cloud (free). You get a tenant like yourname.jfrog.io with Artifactory + Xray. Free tier: ~2 GB storage, 10 GB transfer. Free tier announcement . 2 Log in to the web UI. Explore the two main tabs: Application (browse repos/artifacts) and Administration (create repos, configure Xray). 3 Create your first repositories. Administration → Repositories → + Add Repository . Make a Local Docker repo ( docker-local ), a Remote proxy of Docker Hub ( docker-remote ), and a Virtual repo combining both ( docker ). Repo creation walkthrough . 4 Connect the CLI. Install JFrog CLI (see the CLI tab) then run jf c add and paste your *.jfrog.io URL + a token. Verify with jf rt ping . 5 Push an image. jf docker push yourname.jfrog.io/docker-local/demo:1.0 - then find it in the UI under your local repo. 6 Turn on Xray. Administration → Xray → Indexed Resources → add docker-local . The image scans automatically. 7 Create a Policy + Watch. Administration → Xray → Watches & Policies . Make a Security policy (rule: severity ≥ High, action: notify) and a Watch targeting docker-local . Policies/watches video . 8 Read the results. Open the artifact → Xray tab. Review vulnerabilities by severity, affected components, and fix versions. You've completed the loop. 🎉 Why start here Zero infrastructure to maintain, real cloud UI, and everything in this guide works out of the box. Graduate to local Docker once you want to understand the moving parts. Run the whole platform on your Mac (OrbStack/Docker). You'll see exactly what services make up Artifactory + Xray. Requires Docker Engine 25+ & Docker Compose v2; ARM64 (Apple Silicon) is supported via Docker. Install guide . Heads up on resources The full platform (Artifactory + Xray + PostgreSQL + RabbitMQ) wants ~4 CPU / 8 GB RAM. Your 8 GB Mac setup is the realistic minimum - close other heavy apps. 1 Download the trial Docker-Compose bundle. curl -L "https://releases.jfrog.io/artifactory/jfrog-prox/org/artifactory/pro/docker/jfrog-platform-trial-prox/[RELEASE]/jfrog-platform-trial-prox-[RELEASE]-compose.tar.gz" -o jfrog-compose.tar.gz Source 2 Extract & enter the directory. tar -xvf jfrog-compose.tar.gz cd jfrog-platform-trial-prox-* 3 Run the config script (sets up folder structure + the .env file Compose uses). Follow its prompts. 4 Start everything. docker compose -p trial-pro up -d Give it a few minutes - Xray boots after Artifactory and PostgreSQL are healthy. Xray install steps . 5 Open the UI. Browse to http://localhost:8082 . Default login admin / password - change it immediately. 6 Apply your free license. Paste the license key from your JFrog account to unlock Xray. 7 From here, follow Cloud steps 3-8 - create local/remote/virtual repos, push an image, enable Xray indexing, set a policy + watch, read results. The UI is identical. 8 Tear down cleanly. docker compose -p trial-pro down Add -v only if you want to wipe the data volumes too. Prefer Kubernetes? See Kubernetes / Helm setup for installing Artifactory on a cluster with the JFrog Helm charts. For when you don't want to run a server - just learn the commands & integration patterns. The JFrog CLI binary is jf . One tool drives Artifactory and Xray. CLI summaries . 1 Install the CLI (macOS, Homebrew): brew install jfrog-cli Or one-liner: curl -fL https://install-cli.jfrog.io | sh . Verify: jf --version . 2 Configure a server connection. jf c add my-jpd --url=https://yourname.jfrog.io --interactive Use an access token, not a password. Test: jf rt ping . 3 Scan your source code (shift-left) - no upload needed: jf scan . Scans the dependencies in the current project against Xray. Source-scan docs . 4 Scan a local Docker image: jf docker scan my-app:1.0.0 First run downloads ~100 MB of Xray scanner binaries. jf docker docs . 5 Scan an artifact already in Artifactory, against a watch: jf xr scan --target "docker-local/demo:1.0" --watches "my-watch" Xray CLI commands . 6 Wire it into GitHub Actions (fail the build on Critical issues): - name: Security Scan run: jf build-scan ${{ github.repository }} ${{ github.run_number }} --fail=true --vuln=Critical CI integration . Note: the build must be added under Administration → Xray → Indexed Resources first. Mental model for the CLI jf rt … = Artifactory (uploads, downloads, repos). jf xr … = Xray (scans). jf scan / jf docker scan / jf build-scan = convenient shortcuts that combine both.

title: Kubernetes & Helm setup
body: Skip to content On this page Two distinct goals Install Artifactory on K8s Master & join keys Artifactory as Helm registry OCI push & pull Home / Kubernetes / Helm setup Artifactory Kubernetes How-to Kubernetes & Helm setup There are two distinct things people mean by “JFrog and Kubernetes.” One: run Artifactory itself on a cluster using the official Helm charts. Two: use Artifactory as the Helm/OCI registry your charts live in. This page covers both, in order. Two distinct goals - don't conflate them Orientation Two distinct goals - don't conflate them A Run Artifactory on K8s Deploy the JFrog Platform itself into a Kubernetes cluster using the official Helm charts. This is an infrastructure/install task. B Use Artifactory as a Helm registry Store and serve your own Helm charts (classic or OCI) from an Artifactory repository. This is a day-to-day usage task. Quick glossary before we start Kubernetes (K8s) = a system that runs and manages containers across a cluster of machines. Helm = the "package manager" for Kubernetes; it installs apps from charts (a chart is a bundle of templated Kubernetes config). Namespace = a labelled partition inside a cluster that keeps one app's resources separate from another's. Secret = a Kubernetes object for storing sensitive values (keys, passwords) more safely than plain text. Both rely on Helm, which is why they get confused. Sources: Install Artifactory on Kubernetes , Helm Chart Repositories . Installing Artifactory on Kubernetes with Helm Goal A Installing Artifactory on Kubernetes with Helm JFrog publishes official Helm charts. You add the chart repo, prepare a couple of secrets (master + join keys), then install into a namespace. JFrog - Install Artifactory on Kubernetes . 1) Add the JFrog Helm chart repository helm repo add jfrog https://charts.jfrog.io # register JFrog's chart repo under the name "jfrog" helm repo update # refresh the local list of available charts 2) Install Artifactory into its own namespace # upgrade --install: install (or upgrade) a release named "artifactory" # --set passes the master/join keys; --create-namespace makes the namespace if missing # last arg jfrog/artifactory = which chart to install (repo/chart) helm upgrade --install artifactory \ --set artifactory.masterKey=${MASTER_KEY} \ --set artifactory.joinKey=${JOIN_KEY} \ --namespace artifactory --create-namespace \ jfrog/artifactory upgrade --install is idempotent - running it once or many times reaches the same result: it installs the first time and upgrades thereafter. ("Idempotent" is a key GitOps property: re-running a command is always safe.) For production, supply a values.yaml file (external database, ingress, persistent volumes) instead of bare --set flags. Master & join keys - generate them first Prereq Master & join keys - generate them first The JFrog Platform is made of several small services ("microservices") that must trust each other. They authenticate with a shared join key , and Artifactory encrypts its stored config with a master key . You generate both as random hexadecimal strings and pass them to the chart - ideally as Kubernetes secrets rather than inline on the command line, so they don't leak into your shell history. Generate 32-byte hex keys # openssl makes 32 random bytes as hex; export saves each to an env var export MASTER_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32) export JOIN_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32) # better: store them as Kubernetes secrets (kubectl = the K8s command-line tool) # instead of putting them inline with --set. -n = in the artifactory namespace. kubectl create namespace artifactory kubectl create secret generic my-masterkey \ --from-literal=master-key=${MASTER_KEY} -n artifactory kubectl create secret generic my-joinkey \ --from-literal=join-key=${JOIN_KEY} -n artifactory Why these matter Lose the master key and you can't decrypt your stored configuration; mismatch the join key across nodes and the microservices won't trust each other. Generate once, store securely, reuse on upgrades. title: Two distinct goals - don't conflate them
body: Orientation Two distinct goals - don't conflate them A Run Artifactory on K8s Deploy the JFrog Platform itself into a Kubernetes cluster using the official Helm charts. This is an infrastructure/install task. B Use Artifactory as a Helm registry Store and serve your own Helm charts (classic or OCI) from an Artifactory repository. This is a day-to-day usage task. Quick glossary before we start Kubernetes (K8s) = a system that runs and manages containers across a cluster of machines. Helm = the "package manager" for Kubernetes; it installs apps from charts (a chart is a bundle of templated Kubernetes config). Namespace = a labelled partition inside a cluster that keeps one app's resources separate from another's. Secret = a Kubernetes object for storing sensitive values (keys, passwords) more safely than plain text. Both rely on Helm, which is why they get confused. Sources: Install Artifactory on Kubernetes , Helm Chart Repositories .

title: Installing Artifactory on Kubernetes with Helm
body: Goal A Installing Artifactory on Kubernetes with Helm JFrog publishes official Helm charts. You add the chart repo, prepare a couple of secrets (master + join keys), then install into a namespace. JFrog - Install Artifactory on Kubernetes . 1) Add the JFrog Helm chart repository helm repo add jfrog https://charts.jfrog.io # register JFrog's chart repo under the name "jfrog" helm repo update # refresh the local list of available charts 2) Install Artifactory into its own namespace # upgrade --install: install (or upgrade) a release named "artifactory" # --set passes the master/join keys; --create-namespace makes the namespace if missing # last arg jfrog/artifactory = which chart to install (repo/chart) helm upgrade --install artifactory \ --set artifactory.masterKey=${MASTER_KEY} \ --set artifactory.joinKey=${JOIN_KEY} \ --namespace artifactory --create-namespace \ jfrog/artifactory upgrade --install is idempotent - running it once or many times reaches the same result: it installs the first time and upgrades thereafter. ("Idempotent" is a key GitOps property: re-running a command is always safe.) For production, supply a values.yaml file (external database, ingress, persistent volumes) instead of bare --set flags.

title: Master & join keys - generate them first
body: Prereq Master & join keys - generate them first The JFrog Platform is made of several small services ("microservices") that must trust each other. They authenticate with a shared join key , and Artifactory encrypts its stored config with a master key . You generate both as random hexadecimal strings and pass them to the chart - ideally as Kubernetes secrets rather than inline on the command line, so they don't leak into your shell history. Generate 32-byte hex keys # openssl makes 32 random bytes as hex; export saves each to an env var export MASTER_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32) export JOIN_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32) # better: store them as Kubernetes secrets (kubectl = the K8s command-line tool) # instead of putting them inline with --set. -n = in the artifactory namespace. kubectl create namespace artifactory kubectl create secret generic my-masterkey \ --from-literal=master-key=${MASTER_KEY} -n artifactory kubectl create secret generic my-joinkey \ --from-literal=join-key=${JOIN_KEY} -n artifactory Why these matter Lose the master key and you can't decrypt your stored configuration; mismatch the join key across nodes and the microservices won't trust each other. Generate once, store securely, reuse on upgrades.

title: JFrog Pipelines
body: Skip to content On this page What Pipelines is Core building blocks The YAML DSL First pipeline (steps) Hello World example Home / JFrog Pipelines CI/CD Platform How-to JFrog Pipelines Pipelines is the platform's native CI/CD engine. You declare resources and steps in a YAML DSL stored in your Git repo, connect it through integrations, and Pipelines runs it on build nodes - with Artifactory and Xray built in. What JFrog Pipelines is Concept What JFrog Pipelines is Plain-English glossary for this page CI/CD = Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery: automating the build → test → ship cycle. DSL = Domain-Specific Language: a small, purpose-built language (here, written in YAML) for describing pipelines. Build node = a machine (or container) that actually runs your pipeline steps. YAML = an indentation-based text config format. JFrog Pipelines is a CI/CD automation engine built into the JFrog Platform. You define pipelines in a YAML-based Pipelines DSL - a set of key-value files known as a pipeline config - and store them in a Git-compatible source repository (any repo on GitHub, GitLab, etc.). Pipelines syncs from that source, loads your DSL, and runs continuous integration and delivery on build nodes. JFrog - Creating Pipelines . How is this different from GitHub Actions? Both run YAML-defined CI/CD. Pipelines' edge is native integration with Artifactory (build-info, promotion) and Xray (scanning gates) plus centrally-managed, secure integrations and reusable resources. If you already live in the JFrog platform, Pipelines keeps everything in one place. You can also combine it with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, or Azure DevOps. The core building blocks Building blocks The core building blocks Concept What it is Integration A stored, secure connection to an external service - GitHub, Artifactory, Kubernetes, etc. You provide the URL endpoint and credentials once; Pipelines stores them centrally. Pipeline source The Git repo (added via an integration) that holds your YAML DSL. Pipelines auto-syncs whenever you commit a change to the DSL. Resource A source or destination of data used by the pipeline - e.g. a GitRepo , a BuildInfo , an image. Resources connect steps and can trigger them. Step A unit of execution (e.g. a Bash step). Steps declare inputResources and inputSteps to express dependencies and ordering. Pipeline The named collection of steps and resources that together form a workflow. Sources: JFrog - Pipeline Hello World , Pipelines step-by-step . The YAML DSL: resources + steps DSL The YAML DSL: resources + steps A pipeline config has two main sections. Resources declare the data sources/destinations; pipelines declare the steps that act on them. Steps wire together via inputSteps (run after) and inputResources (consume/produce). JFrog - Workflow examples . A step that runs after another step succeeds pipelines: - name: demo_pipeline steps: - name: build_step type: Bash configuration: inputResources: - name: my_git_repo # a GitRepo resource execution: onExecute: - echo "building…" - name: publish_step type: Bash configuration: inputSteps: - name: build_step # runs only after build_step execution: onExecute: - echo "publishing build-info to Artifactory" Standing up your first pipeline How-to Standing up your first pipeline Sign in to the JFrog Platform with your Artifactory credentials. On JFrog Cloud, a default dynamic node pool is provisioned for you (a "node pool" is a group of build machines that scale up/down on demand), so you already have build nodes - nothing to install. Add a GitHub integration. Administration → Pipelines → Integrations → add a GitHub integration with a personal access token (a GitHub token that grants API access) that has the repo + admin scopes (a "scope" is a permission the token is allowed to use). Add a pipeline source. Point Pipelines at the repo (and branch) holding your YAML DSL, connected through that integration. Pipelines syncs and processes the DSL. Commit DSL changes. Every commit to the YAML re-syncs the source and reloads the pipeline. Run it. Trigger manually or via a GitRepo resource that fires steps when the repo changes. Source: JFrog - Pipeline Example: Hello World . The "Hello World" example, end to end Worked example The "Hello World" example, end to end 1 Fork example repo jfrog-pipelines-simple-example → 2 Add GitHub integration token w/ repo+admin scope → 3 Add pipeline source points at the YAML → 4 Sync & run GitRepo trigger fires steps The official Hello World demonstrates a GitHub integration, a pipeline source, a GitRepo trigger, and inputResources / inputSteps dependencies. Fork the JFrog example repo, update the path values, commit, and watch it run. JFrog - Hello World . Where Artifactory & Xray plug in Add a BuildInfo resource to publish build-info to Artifactory, then a promotion step (see Build promotion ) gated by an Xray scan. That turns a plain CI pipeline into a secured, traceable delivery pipeline - the whole point of doing CI inside JFrog. ← Previous Frogbot & IDE Next → Access tokens & permissions All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: What JFrog Pipelines is
body: Concept What JFrog Pipelines is Plain-English glossary for this page CI/CD = Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery: automating the build → test → ship cycle. DSL = Domain-Specific Language: a small, purpose-built language (here, written in YAML) for describing pipelines. Build node = a machine (or container) that actually runs your pipeline steps. YAML = an indentation-based text config format. JFrog Pipelines is a CI/CD automation engine built into the JFrog Platform. You define pipelines in a YAML-based Pipelines DSL - a set of key-value files known as a pipeline config - and store them in a Git-compatible source repository (any repo on GitHub, GitLab, etc.). Pipelines syncs from that source, loads your DSL, and runs continuous integration and delivery on build nodes. JFrog - Creating Pipelines . How is this different from GitHub Actions? Both run YAML-defined CI/CD. Pipelines' edge is native integration with Artifactory (build-info, promotion) and Xray (scanning gates) plus centrally-managed, secure integrations and reusable resources. If you already live in the JFrog platform, Pipelines keeps everything in one place. You can also combine it with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, or Azure DevOps.

title: The core building blocks
body: Building blocks The core building blocks Concept What it is Integration A stored, secure connection to an external service - GitHub, Artifactory, Kubernetes, etc. You provide the URL endpoint and credentials once; Pipelines stores them centrally. Pipeline source The Git repo (added via an integration) that holds your YAML DSL. Pipelines auto-syncs whenever you commit a change to the DSL. Resource A source or destination of data used by the pipeline - e.g. a GitRepo , a BuildInfo , an image. Resources connect steps and can trigger them. Step A unit of execution (e.g. a Bash step). Steps declare inputResources and inputSteps to express dependencies and ordering. Pipeline The named collection of steps and resources that together form a workflow. title: The YAML DSL: resources + steps
body: DSL The YAML DSL: resources + steps A pipeline config has two main sections. Resources declare the data sources/destinations; pipelines declare the steps that act on them. Steps wire together via inputSteps (run after) and inputResources (consume/produce). JFrog - Workflow examples . A step that runs after another step succeeds pipelines: - name: demo_pipeline steps: - name: build_step type: Bash configuration: inputResources: - name: my_git_repo # a GitRepo resource execution: onExecute: - echo "building…" - name: publish_step type: Bash configuration: inputSteps: - name: build_step # runs only after build_step execution: onExecute: - echo "publishing build-info to Artifactory"

title: Standing up your first pipeline
body: How-to Standing up your first pipeline Sign in to the JFrog Platform with your Artifactory credentials. On JFrog Cloud, a default dynamic node pool is provisioned for you (a "node pool" is a group of build machines that scale up/down on demand), so you already have build nodes - nothing to install. Add a GitHub integration. Administration → Pipelines → Integrations → add a GitHub integration with a personal access token (a GitHub token that grants API access) that has the repo + admin scopes (a "scope" is a permission the token is allowed to use). Add a pipeline source. Point Pipelines at the repo (and branch) holding your YAML DSL, connected through that integration. Pipelines syncs and processes the DSL. Commit DSL changes. Every commit to the YAML re-syncs the source and reloads the pipeline. Run it. Trigger manually or via a GitRepo resource that fires steps when the repo changes. Source: JFrog - Pipeline Example: Hello World .
