title: Release Bundles & Distribution
body: Skip to content On this page The problem What a Release Bundle is Bundle lifecycle Distribution & edge nodes Create & distribute (CLI) Bundle vs. promotion Home / Release Bundles & Distribution Artifactory Supply chain Deep-dive Release Bundles & Distribution A Release Bundle is a sealed, signed manifest of exactly which artifacts make up a release. JFrog Distribution then ships that bundle out to edge nodes and remote sites - so what arrives is provably identical to what you signed. The problem Release Bundles solve Concept The problem Release Bundles solve Promotion moves binaries inside one platform. But shipping a release out - to a customer site, an edge location, or an air-gapped network (a network with no internet connection at all, for security) - raises new questions: which exact files belong to "v2.4.0"? Were any swapped in transit? Can the receiving side prove provenance (a verifiable record of where each file came from and that it wasn't altered)? A loose folder of artifacts answers none of these. A Release Bundle is the answer: an immutable (can never be changed after creation), signed specification of the precise set of artifacts that make up a release, with a checksum-verified manifest (the packing list - it names every file and its checksum fingerprint). JFrog - Release Lifecycle Management . What's inside a Release Bundle Definition What's inside a Release Bundle A curated artifact set. You select the artifacts that make up the release - by build, by file path, or by an AQL query (Artifactory Query Language, a JSON-based search language for finding artifacts by name, property, repo, etc.). The bundle records each one's checksum. Immutability. Once created, the bundle's contents can't change. A new release means a new bundle version. Cryptographic signing. The bundle manifest is signed with a GPG key (GPG = GNU Privacy Guard, a standard tool for cryptographic signatures). The signer holds a private key; recipients hold the matching public key and use it to confirm the bundle came from you and wasn't tampered with. Provenance metadata. The bundle carries the source build-info and (optionally) Xray security-scan results, giving the receiver evidence of exactly what was built and what was vetted. 📜 One-sentence analogy A Release Bundle is a tamper-evident shipping manifest with a wax seal . It lists every item in the crate, and the seal proves nobody opened it between the warehouse and the customer's loading dock. The bundle lifecycle Flow The bundle lifecycle 1 Create select artifacts → sign → 2 Sign GPG-signed manifest → 3 Distribute push to edge nodes → 4 Verify & consume checksum-checked on arrival The bundle is created and sealed once, then distributed to one or many targets. Each target verifies signatures and checksums before exposing the artifacts. JFrog - Distributing Release Bundles . Distribution & edge nodes Distribution Distribution & edge nodes JFrog Distribution is the service that takes a signed Release Bundle and delivers it to edge nodes - lightweight Artifactory instances positioned close to consumers (a factory floor, a CDN POP, an air-gapped enclave). Edge nodes are read-only consumption points: they receive distributed bundles but aren't general-purpose repositories. JFrog Distribution docs . Component Role Distribution service Orchestrates creating, signing, and shipping bundles to targets. Edge node A consumption-only Artifactory at the destination that hosts distributed bundles. Distribution target The rule selecting which edge node(s) a given bundle goes to. Why edge nodes matter Consumers at the edge pull from a nearby node - fast and resilient - while you keep a single signed source of truth centrally. Air-gapped sites receive the bundle as a verifiable package rather than opening a live connection to your main platform. Create and distribute a bundle (CLI) CLI Create and distribute a bundle (CLI) The JFrog CLI exposes release-bundle commands under jf rbc / jf rbu (create/update) and jf rbd (distribute) - these short names are just aliases for the longer commands below. The conceptual flow: JFrog CLI - Release Bundles . Create a signed bundle from a spec, then distribute # 1. Create release bundle "my-app" v2.4.0 from a spec file. # --spec: JSON listing which artifacts to include (by path/AQL) # --signing-key: the GPG key that signs (seals) the manifest jf release-bundle-create my-app 2.4.0 \ --spec=release-spec.json \ --signing-key=my-gpg-key # 2. Distribute it to every edge node matching the pattern (* is a wildcard) jf release-bundle-distribute my-app 2.4.0 \ --site="edge-eu-*" The spec selects artifacts (by AQL/path); the signing key seals the manifest; the distribute step fans it out to matching edge sites. Exact subcommands vary by Release Lifecycle Management version - check the linked CLI reference for your platform. Release Bundle vs. build promotion Compare Release Bundle vs. build promotion Aspect Build promotion Release Bundle + Distribution Scope Within one platform Across platforms / out to edges Mutability Status changes over time Immutable once signed Integrity proof Checksum tracking GPG-signed manifest Primary goal Advance maturity stage Ship a sealed release safely Air-gap friendly No Yes They compose: promote a build to release-local with an Xray gate (see Build promotion ), then bundle and distribute that vetted set to the edge. ← Previous Build promotion Next → Xray Policies & Watches All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Replication & Federation
body: Skip to content On this page Why sync at all Replication Push vs. pull Federated repositories Which to choose How to set it up Home / Replication & Federation Artifactory Multi-site Deep-dive Replication & Federation Once you run JFrog in more than one location, you need copies of your artifacts to stay in sync. JFrog gives you two mechanisms - classic replication and federated repositories. They solve overlapping problems differently. Why sync artifacts across sites at all? Concept Why sync artifacts across sites at all? A single Artifactory instance is one point in the world. The moment you have teams in London and Tokyo, a CI (Continuous Integration - the automated server that builds and tests your code) farm in another region, or a disaster-recovery site (a backup copy you can switch to if your main one fails), builds in one place need the same binaries that live in another. "Binaries" here just means the packaged outputs you store in Artifactory - Docker images, JAR files, npm packages, and so on. Pulling all of them across an ocean on every build is slow and fragile. 01 Latency Developers and CI resolve dependencies from a local copy instead of crossing continents on every download. 02 Availability If one site or its network goes down, the others still have their own copy and keep building. 03 Disaster recovery A synced standby instance lets you fail over with the artifacts already in place. First, one term you'll see everywhere: JPD A JPD stands for JFrog Platform Deployment - it's simply one complete, self-contained install of the JFrog Platform (Artifactory + Xray + the rest), running in one place. When this page says "site", "instance", or "deployment", it means one JPD. Syncing artifacts across sites means syncing them between JPDs. JFrog offers two answers: replication (an older, one-directional copy job) and federation (newer, automatic, multi-directional mirroring). "One-directional" means data flows from a source to a target only; "multi-directional" means every copy can both send and receive. JFrog - Repository Replication . Replication - copying repositories between instances Replication Replication - copying repositories between instances Replication synchronizes the contents of a repository on one Artifactory instance with a repository on another. It runs on a schedule - defined by a cron expression, which is just a compact string that says "run at these times" (for example, every hour) - or it can be triggered automatically as an event the moment something changes. It copies artifacts plus their properties (key/value tags attached to an artifact, like stage=released ). Replication works on local and remote repositories. Source . Key trait: it's one-directional A single replication has a clear source and target. Site A pushes to Site B, or Site B pulls from Site A - but one replication relationship only flows one way. To mirror both directions you configure two separate jobs. Push vs. pull replication Replication Push vs. pull replication Mode Who initiates How it works Typical use Push The source instance The source repo pushes new/changed artifacts out to a target repo on a remote instance. A central build site fanning releases out to regional mirrors. Pull The target instance A remote repo's "replication" pulls content from its upstream Artifactory on a schedule. An edge/DR site keeping itself current from a master. A Source JPD has the artifacts → push → B Target JPD receives a copy - or, reversed initiator - A Source JPD has the artifacts ← pull ← B Target JPD fetches the copy Push and pull move data the same direction (A → B); they differ only in which side starts the transfer . Push is good when the source can reach the target; pull is good when only the target can reach out (for example, a firewalled edge site that is allowed to make outbound connections but not receive inbound ones). JFrog Replication . Federated repositories - automatic multi-directional mirroring Federation Federated repositories - automatic multi-directional mirroring A Federated repository behaves like a local repo, but it is bi-directionally mirrored across multiple members - up to 10 repositories living on different JFrog Platform Deployments (JPDs, defined above). "Bi-directional" means the sync flows both ways. Write to any member and the change propagates (spreads automatically) to all the others, asynchronously (in the background, so your write doesn't have to wait for every copy to finish). JFrog - Federated Repositories . Bi-directional by design. Every member is both source and target - no manual two-way setup. Deploy to London, it appears in Tokyo, and vice-versa. Async, queue-backed. Changes are replicated through a persistent queue, so a temporary network blip doesn't lose data - it catches up when connectivity returns. Configuration is synced too. Repository configuration (not just artifacts) is kept aligned across members. Up to 10 members. A federation can span up to ten member repositories across JPDs. Enterprise tier. Federated repositories require an Enterprise X (or higher) paid subscription - so you won't see them on the free tier. Federation is the modern default for "active-active" multi-site (every site is live and writable at once); replication still fits one-way disaster-recovery (DR) standbys and edge-cache patterns. Which one should you use? Decision Which one should you use? Setting each one up How-to Setting each one up Configure replication (UI) Open the repo. Administration → Repositories → select a local or remote repo → Replications tab. Add a replication. Provide the target instance URL, the target repository key, and credentials (use an access token). Pick a schedule. Set a cron expression (e.g. every hour) and choose whether to enable event-based replication for near-real-time push. Save & test. Run it once manually and confirm artifacts land in the target. Create a federated repository (UI) Add repository → Federated. Choose the package type, then create the repo on your first JPD. Add members. In the Federated configuration, add member URLs for the other JPDs. For one JPD to trust another, they must first be linked - JFrog calls this the "circle of trust", set up through Mission Control (JFrog's tool for managing multiple deployments). Think of it as the two deployments exchanging IDs so they'll accept each other's data. Deploy anywhere. Push an artifact to any member; verify it appears on the others within seconds. Tip for your labs Prerequisite reminder: on the free single-instance tier you can't truly demonstrate multi-JPD federation - you need at least two separate deployments. You can still create the repo types and read the configuration screens to understand the model. For a real two-site test, stand up two self-hosted instances ("self-hosted" means you run Artifactory yourself, e.g. in Docker, instead of using JFrog's cloud) - see the Docker lab on the Fundamentals page. ← Previous Fundamentals Next → Build promotion All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Why sync artifacts across sites at all?
body: Concept Why sync artifacts across sites at all? A single Artifactory instance is one point in the world. The moment you have teams in London and Tokyo, a CI (Continuous Integration - the automated server that builds and tests your code) farm in another region, or a disaster-recovery site (a backup copy you can switch to if your main one fails), builds in one place need the same binaries that live in another. "Binaries" here just means the packaged outputs you store in Artifactory - Docker images, JAR files, npm packages, and so on. Pulling all of them across an ocean on every build is slow and fragile. 01 Latency Developers and CI resolve dependencies from a local copy instead of crossing continents on every download. 02 Availability If one site or its network goes down, the others still have their own copy and keep building. 03 Disaster recovery A synced standby instance lets you fail over with the artifacts already in place. First, one term you'll see everywhere: JPD A JPD stands for JFrog Platform Deployment - it's simply one complete, self-contained install of the JFrog Platform (Artifactory + Xray + the rest), running in one place. When this page says "site", "instance", or "deployment", it means one JPD. Syncing artifacts across sites means syncing them between JPDs. JFrog offers two answers: replication (an older, one-directional copy job) and federation (newer, automatic, multi-directional mirroring). "One-directional" means data flows from a source to a target only; "multi-directional" means every copy can both send and receive. JFrog - Repository Replication .

title: Replication - copying repositories between instances
body: Replication Replication - copying repositories between instances Replication synchronizes the contents of a repository on one Artifactory instance with a repository on another. It runs on a schedule - defined by a cron expression, which is just a compact string that says "run at these times" (for example, every hour) - or it can be triggered automatically as an event the moment something changes. It copies artifacts plus their properties (key/value tags attached to an artifact, like stage=released ). Replication works on local and remote repositories. Source . Key trait: it's one-directional A single replication has a clear source and target. Site A pushes to Site B, or Site B pulls from Site A - but one replication relationship only flows one way. To mirror both directions you configure two separate jobs.

title: Setting each one up
body: How-to Setting each one up Configure replication (UI) Open the repo. Administration → Repositories → select a local or remote repo → Replications tab. Add a replication. Provide the target instance URL, the target repository key, and credentials (use an access token). Pick a schedule. Set a cron expression (e.g. every hour) and choose whether to enable event-based replication for near-real-time push. Save & test. Run it once manually and confirm artifacts land in the target. Create a federated repository (UI) Add repository → Federated. Choose the package type, then create the repo on your first JPD. Add members. In the Federated configuration, add member URLs for the other JPDs. For one JPD to trust another, they must first be linked - JFrog calls this the "circle of trust", set up through Mission Control (JFrog's tool for managing multiple deployments). Think of it as the two deployments exchanging IDs so they'll accept each other's data. Deploy anywhere. Push an artifact to any member; verify it appears on the others within seconds. Tip for your labs Prerequisite reminder: on the free single-instance tier you can't truly demonstrate multi-JPD federation - you need at least two separate deployments. You can still create the repo types and read the configuration screens to understand the model. For a real two-site test, stand up two self-hosted instances ("self-hosted" means you run Artifactory yourself, e.g. in Docker, instead of using JFrog's cloud) - see the Docker lab on the Fundamentals page. ← Previous Fundamentals Next → Build promotion All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: The Artifactory REST API
body: Skip to content On this page Authentication Key endpoints Calling with curl The jf api shortcut Create a repo via API Best practices Home / Artifactory REST API Artifactory Integration Reference The Artifactory REST API Everything you can click in the UI, you can automate over HTTP. This page covers how to authenticate, the endpoints you'll actually use, and how to call them from curl and the JFrog CLI's built-in API shortcut. Authentication - three ways in Foundation Authentication - three ways in New to APIs? Start here An API (Application Programming Interface) is a way for programs to talk to Artifactory instead of a human clicking the web UI. REST is the most common style of web API: you send an HTTP request (the same protocol your browser uses) to a URL called an endpoint , using a method that states your intent - GET (read), POST (create), PUT (create/replace), DELETE (remove). Artifactory replies with JSON (a simple text format for structured data). That's the whole idea: a URL + a method + (sometimes) a JSON body. Every Artifactory REST call needs to identify you (this is authentication - proving who you are). There are three accepted methods; pick access tokens for automation. JFrog - Intro to the REST APIs . Method How it's sent Use when Access token (recommended) Authorization: Bearer <token> - an HTTP header you attach to the request; "Bearer" just means "whoever carries this token is allowed in" Any automation - scoped, revocable, no password exposure. Basic auth -u user:password Quick local tests only; avoid in scripts. API key X-JFrog-Art-Api: <key> Legacy integrations (being phased out in favor of tokens). See the Access tokens & permissions page for how to mint and scope a token. The endpoints you'll actually use Reference The endpoints you'll actually use Endpoint Method Does /artifactory/api/system/ping GET Health check - returns OK . /artifactory/api/repositories GET List all repositories. /artifactory/api/repositories/<key> PUT Create/configure a repository. /artifactory/api/repositories/<key> GET Read one repo's config. /artifactory/api/storage/<repo>/<path> GET File/folder info & metadata. /artifactory/api/build/promote/<name>/<num> POST Promote a build (see Build promotion). /access/api/v1/users/<username> GET/PUT Manage a user. /access/api/v1/groups/<group> GET/PUT Manage a group. /access/api/v1/tokens POST Create an access token. Note the two base paths: /artifactory/api/... for repository/storage operations, and /access/api/... for identity (users, groups, tokens). Sources: JFrog REST APIs , REST quickstart . Calling the API with curl How-to Calling the API with curl curl is a built-in command-line tool for making HTTP requests. -H attaches a header; $JF_TOKEN reads your token from an environment variable so it never appears in the command itself. Health check # -H attaches the auth header; the URL is the endpoint we call curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/system/ping # → the server replies: OK List every repository as JSON # jq (a JSON tool) pretty-prints the reply curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/repositories | jq . Put the token in an env var Never paste tokens inline in shared scripts. Export JF_TOKEN once (ideally from a secret manager) and reference it. Rotate it on a schedule. The jf api shortcut Shortcut The jf api shortcut If you've already configured a server with jf c add ("c" = config; this stores your server URL and token once), the CLI can call any Artifactory REST endpoint using those stored credentials - so you never type an auth header again. JFrog - Use API endpoints via CLI . Same "list repositories" call, authenticated automatically jf rt curl -XGET /api/repositories # or the generic platform call: jf api /artifactory/api/repositories This is the fastest way to script against Artifactory during your labs: configure once, then drive any endpoint without juggling tokens in every command. Create a local Docker repo via the API Worked example Create a local Docker repo via the API PUT a repository configuration # -XPUT = PUT method (create/replace). The URL ends in the new repo's key (name). # -d sends the JSON body below. rclass = repo class (local/remote/virtual). curl -XPUT -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/repositories/docker-local \ -d '{ "rclass": "local", "packageType": "docker" }' Swap "rclass": "remote" plus a "url" field for a proxy repo, or "virtual" with a "repositories" list to aggregate several repos behind one. This is exactly what the UI's "Add Repository" form does under the hood. (If "local / remote / virtual" is new, see the Fundamentals page.) Best practices Guidance Best practices Use access tokens, never passwords in automation - they're scoped and revocable. Prefer jf api / jf rt curl in CLI contexts so credentials stay in CLI config, not your shell history. Check /system/ping first in scripts to fail fast on connectivity/auth problems. Treat config-as-code. Keep repo-creation JSON in version control so your platform is reproducible. Rotate tokens and scope them to the minimum permission target needed (see Access tokens ). ← Previous Xray Policies & Watches Next → Frogbot & IDE All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Authentication - three ways in
body: Foundation Authentication - three ways in New to APIs? Start here An API (Application Programming Interface) is a way for programs to talk to Artifactory instead of a human clicking the web UI. REST is the most common style of web API: you send an HTTP request (the same protocol your browser uses) to a URL called an endpoint , using a method that states your intent - GET (read), POST (create), PUT (create/replace), DELETE (remove). Artifactory replies with JSON (a simple text format for structured data). That's the whole idea: a URL + a method + (sometimes) a JSON body. Every Artifactory REST call needs to identify you (this is authentication - proving who you are). There are three accepted methods; pick access tokens for automation. JFrog - Intro to the REST APIs . Method How it's sent Use when Access token (recommended) Authorization: Bearer <token> - an HTTP header you attach to the request; "Bearer" just means "whoever carries this token is allowed in" Any automation - scoped, revocable, no password exposure. Basic auth -u user:password Quick local tests only; avoid in scripts. API key X-JFrog-Art-Api: <key> Legacy integrations (being phased out in favor of tokens). See the Access tokens & permissions page for how to mint and scope a token.

title: The endpoints you'll actually use
body: Reference The endpoints you'll actually use Endpoint Method Does /artifactory/api/system/ping GET Health check - returns OK . /artifactory/api/repositories GET List all repositories. /artifactory/api/repositories/<key> PUT Create/configure a repository. /artifactory/api/repositories/<key> GET Read one repo's config. /artifactory/api/storage/<repo>/<path> GET File/folder info & metadata. /artifactory/api/build/promote/<name>/<num> POST Promote a build (see Build promotion). /access/api/v1/users/<username> GET/PUT Manage a user. /access/api/v1/groups/<group> GET/PUT Manage a group. /access/api/v1/tokens POST Create an access token. Note the two base paths: /artifactory/api/... for repository/storage operations, and /access/api/... for identity (users, groups, tokens). Sources: JFrog REST APIs , REST quickstart .

title: Calling the API with curl
body: How-to Calling the API with curl curl is a built-in command-line tool for making HTTP requests. -H attaches a header; $JF_TOKEN reads your token from an environment variable so it never appears in the command itself. Health check # -H attaches the auth header; the URL is the endpoint we call curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/system/ping # → the server replies: OK List every repository as JSON # jq (a JSON tool) pretty-prints the reply curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/repositories | jq . Put the token in an env var Never paste tokens inline in shared scripts. Export JF_TOKEN once (ideally from a secret manager) and reference it. Rotate it on a schedule.

title: The jf api shortcut
body: Shortcut The jf api shortcut If you've already configured a server with jf c add ("c" = config; this stores your server URL and token once), the CLI can call any Artifactory REST endpoint using those stored credentials - so you never type an auth header again. JFrog - Use API endpoints via CLI . Same "list repositories" call, authenticated automatically jf rt curl -XGET /api/repositories # or the generic platform call: jf api /artifactory/api/repositories This is the fastest way to script against Artifactory during your labs: configure once, then drive any endpoint without juggling tokens in every command.

title: Create a local Docker repo via the API
body: Worked example Create a local Docker repo via the API PUT a repository configuration # -XPUT = PUT method (create/replace). The URL ends in the new repo's key (name). # -d sends the JSON body below. rclass = repo class (local/remote/virtual). curl -XPUT -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/repositories/docker-local \ -d '{ "rclass": "local", "packageType": "docker" }' Swap "rclass": "remote" plus a "url" field for a proxy repo, or "virtual" with a "repositories" list to aggregate several repos behind one. This is exactly what the UI's "Add Repository" form does under the hood. (If "local / remote / virtual" is new, see the Fundamentals page.)

title: Best practices
body: Guidance Best practices Use access tokens, never passwords in automation - they're scoped and revocable. Prefer jf api / jf rt curl in CLI contexts so credentials stay in CLI config, not your shell history. Check /system/ping first in scripts to fail fast on connectivity/auth problems. Treat config-as-code. Keep repo-creation JSON in version control so your platform is reproducible. title: Xray Policies & Watches
body: Skip to content On this page 30-second recap The enforcement model The 3 policy types Writing security rules Writing license rules Operational-risk rules Enforcement actions Watches: the scope Lab A: build a policy Lab B: build a watch How this connects Recap Sources Home / Xray Policies & Watches Xray Security Hands-on Xray Policies & Watches Finding a vulnerability is only half the job - the other half is acting on it automatically. This page is the hands-on layer: how to write the rules inside a Policy, choose what happens when a rule is broken, then create a Watch and point it at your repositories. If the words Policy and Watch are new, read the short Policies & Watches primer on Fundamentals first; here we go deeper and actually build them. 30-second recap (the two objects) Recap 30-second recap (the two objects) Xray's enforcement rests on two linked objects. The Fundamentals page explains the concept in full; here is the one-line version so this page stands on its own. A Policy is the law : a set of rules that say what counts as a problem and what to do about it. A Watch is the jurisdiction and the police : it says where the law applies and switches enforcement on. A finding only becomes a violation when a Watch points a Policy at the place the finding lives. JFrog - Policy and Governance . One term before we start: violation A violation is the record Xray creates when an artifact actually breaks a rule that a Watch has switched on. No Watch, no violation - even if the vulnerability is real and indexed. That is the single most common point of confusion, so keep it in mind as you read. The enforcement model, end to end Mental model The enforcement model, end to end Every piece below has exactly one job. Read the chain left to right: Xray indexes an artifact, a Watch decides it is in scope, the Watch's Policy checks its rules, and if a rule's conditions are met a violation is created and its actions fire. 1 Indexed artifact component graph, scanned → 2 Watch is it in scope? → 3 Policy + rules conditions met? if a rule matches 4 Violation the record → 5 Actions notify / fail / block The Policy holds the rules; the Watch supplies the scope. You build them in either order, but nothing is enforced until a Watch ties a Policy to a resource. JFrog - Watches in Xray . The three policy types Policy types The three policy types When you create a Policy you pick exactly one type. The type decides which rule conditions you can configure. You cannot mix a license rule into a security policy - you would create one policy of each type and attach both to the same Watch. Type What it catches Typical rule Security Known vulnerabilities (CVEs) and malicious packages in your components. Block anything with a High or Critical CVE that has a fix available. License Compliance Open-source licenses your organization bans or must track. Fail the build if a component uses GPL-3.0. Operational Risk Components that are outdated, deprecated, end-of-life, or poorly maintained. Alert if a dependency has not been updated in 12+ months. Source: JFrog - Create Policies . Writing a security rule Writing rules Writing a security rule A rule is a condition plus the actions to take when it matches. A security policy gives you two ways to define the condition - by how severe the vulnerability is, or by its exact CVSS score. CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) is the industry-standard 0-10 number that rates how dangerous a flaw is; higher is worse. By minimal severity Pick the lowest severity that should trip the rule. Everything at that level or higher matches. Critical High Medium Low All Example: minimal severity = High catches both High and Critical, ignores Medium and below. By CVSS range Set a numeric band, for example CVSS 7.0 to 10.0, when you want finer control than the named buckets give you. Useful when your org's risk threshold sits between two severity labels. Two extra conditions sharpen a security rule so you alert on what is actually actionable: Fix version available. Only flag vulnerabilities that have a released fix, so developers always have somewhere to go. This dramatically cuts noise. Malicious package. Trip on packages JFrog's security research has flagged as deliberately malicious (for example, typosquats), regardless of CVSS. A good first security rule Minimal severity = High , with fix version available turned on, action = create a violation plus notify . It is loud enough to matter, quiet enough to act on, and blocks nothing until you trust it. Tighten to block download later. Source: JFrog - Create Policies (Security Policy Rules) . Writing a license rule Writing rules Writing a license rule A license policy enforces which open-source licenses are allowed in your software. You define the condition as one of two lists. Approach How it works When to use it Banned licenses List the licenses you forbid (e.g. GPL-3.0, AGPL-3.0). A component carrying any of them trips the rule. You only need to block a handful of known-problematic licenses. Allowed licenses List the only licenses you permit (e.g. MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD-3-Clause). Anything outside the list trips the rule. Strict shops that want an explicit allow-list - safer by default. Why a single component can carry several licenses Open-source packages are sometimes dual-licensed or bundle code under more than one license. Xray evaluates every license it detects on a component, so a rule can trip on one license even when others are fine. Build your list with that in mind. Source: JFrog - Policy and Governance (license rules) . Operational-risk rules Writing rules Operational-risk rules Operational risk is about maintainability , not security - components that are stale or abandoned are a liability even when no CVE exists yet. JFrog's documented examples make the intent concrete: Staleness. Alert developers if a dependency has not been updated in 12+ months. End-of-life. Fail builds if a package is flagged as end-of-life. Deprecation. Block downloads of deprecated components. Source: JFrog - Create Policies (Operational Risk Rule) . What a rule can do: enforcement actions Enforcement What a rule can do: enforcement actions Each rule, when matched, always records a violation . On top of that you choose any number of automatic actions. They fall into two families: notify (tell someone) and block (stop the artifact from moving). Action Family Effect Generate a violation Always on The record that something broke a rule. Everything else is optional on top. Notify watch recipients / developer / email list Notify Email the watch owner, the resource's developer, or a configured address list. Trigger a webhook Notify Call an external endpoint (Slack, a pipeline, your own service) when the rule is violated. Create a Jira ticket Notify Open a tracking ticket automatically for each violation. Fail builds Block Make a CI build fail when the rule is violated. Supports a grace period. Block downloads Block Stop the artifact from being downloaded from Artifactory. Supports a grace period. Block Release Bundle promotion / distribution Block Stop a Release Bundle from being promoted or distributed in the release lifecycle. Supports a grace period. Grace periods: how to roll out blocking without an outage The three blocking actions let you set a grace period - a window where the violation is recorded and teams are warned, but the block does not yet bite. This is the safe way to introduce enforcement: start in notify-only, switch on blocking with a grace period, then let the grace period expire once teams have caught up. JFrog - Create Policies (enforcement actions) . Watches: choosing the scope Scope Watches: choosing the scope A Watch answers one question: which resources do these policies apply to? When you add resources to a Watch you choose from four kinds of target. A Watch must have at least one policy attached, or it enforces nothing. Resource What it scopes Example Repositories Everything stored in one or more repos. Watch docker-prod-local for Critical CVEs. Builds The artifacts and dependencies captured in named builds. Watch the payments-api build to gate CI. Release Bundles The signed, immutable bundles you promote and distribute. Block distribution of a bundle with a vulnerable base layer. Projects All resources grouped under a JFrog Project. Apply one compliance baseline across a whole team's project. Best practice: one Watch per environment JFrog recommends separate Watches for different environments - a permissive, notify-only Watch on dev repos and a strict, blocking Watch on production repos - so the same finding is handled appropriately for where it lives. JFrog - Create Watches (best practices) . Lab A: create a security policy Lab A Lab A: create a security policy Goal: a policy that records and notifies on High/Critical vulnerabilities that have a fix, blocking nothing yet. This is the exact click-path from the JFrog docs. 1 Open Watches & Policies. In the JFrog Platform, go to Xray → Watches & Policies . 2 New Policy. Click New Policy and enter a name, for example prod-security-baseline . Add a short description of its purpose. 3 Pick the type. Choose Security Policy . 4 Add a rule. Click Add Rule . Set the rule category to By Minimal Severity = High , and enable Fix version available so you only flag actionable findings. 5 Set actions. Leave the default violation on, and add Notify the watch recipient . Do not enable blocking yet - you will tighten later. 6 Save. Save the policy. It now exists but enforces nothing until a Watch uses it - that is Lab B. Source: JFrog - Create Policies . Lab B: create a watch and attach the policy Lab B Lab B: create a watch and attach the policy Goal: point the policy from Lab A at a real repository so findings start becoming violations. 1 New Watch. Still under Xray → Watches & Policies , click New Watch and give it a unique name, for example prod-repos-watch . 2 Add resources. Click Add Resources and select what to monitor - a Repository (e.g. your production Docker repo), a Build , a Release Bundle , or a Project . Click Apply . 3 Attach the policy. Link the prod-security-baseline policy from Lab A. A Watch can carry several policies - add a license policy here too if you have one. 4 Save & apply. Save the Watch. Xray evaluates the in-scope resources against the attached policies and starts generating violations on matches. 5 Verify. Open the Watch and review its violations. Seeing the High/Critical findings you expected confirms the policy and scope line up. Reminder you will thank yourself for If you created the policy but see no violations, check that a Watch actually attaches it to the right resource. A policy with no Watch is inert by design. Source: JFrog - Create Watches . How this connects to the rest of the platform Connections How this connects to the rest of the platform Policies and Watches are the engine; other pages show where that engine plugs in across the software lifecycle: Shift left. The same policies drive earlier feedback - in the IDE and on pull requests - covered on Frogbot & IDE . Promotion gates. A Watch on a staging repo can block promotion to release when a High/Critical CVE is present - see the worked example on Build promotion . The basics. If indexing, scanning, or the Policy-vs-Watch split still feel fuzzy, the Fundamentals page covers them from zero. Recap Recap Policy = rules, Watch = scope. Nothing is enforced until a Watch attaches a Policy to a resource; the result of a match is a violation . Three policy types. Security (CVEs, malicious), License (banned or allowed lists), Operational risk (stale, EOL, deprecated). One type per policy; attach several policies to one Watch. Sharpen security rules with minimal severity or a CVSS range, plus fix-available to cut noise. Actions are notify (email, webhook, Jira) or block (fail build, block download, block bundle promotion). Blocking actions support a grace period for safe rollout. Watches target repositories, builds, release bundles, or projects. Use one Watch per environment. Sources / further reading Sources & further reading Sources / further reading JFrog Docs - Create Policies (policy types, rule conditions, enforcement actions, grace periods). JFrog Docs - Create Watches (resources, attaching policies, best practices). JFrog Docs - Watches in JFrog Xray (what a Watch is and what it targets). JFrog Help - Policy and Governance (policies across the SDLC, best practices). ← Previous Release Bundles & Distribution Next → Artifactory REST API All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: 30-second recap (the two objects)
body: Recap 30-second recap (the two objects) Xray's enforcement rests on two linked objects. The Fundamentals page explains the concept in full; here is the one-line version so this page stands on its own. A Policy is the law : a set of rules that say what counts as a problem and what to do about it. A Watch is the jurisdiction and the police : it says where the law applies and switches enforcement on. A finding only becomes a violation when a Watch points a Policy at the place the finding lives. JFrog - Policy and Governance . One term before we start: violation A violation is the record Xray creates when an artifact actually breaks a rule that a Watch has switched on. No Watch, no violation - even if the vulnerability is real and indexed. That is the single most common point of confusion, so keep it in mind as you read.

title: The enforcement model, end to end
body: Mental model The enforcement model, end to end Every piece below has exactly one job. Read the chain left to right: Xray indexes an artifact, a Watch decides it is in scope, the Watch's Policy checks its rules, and if a rule's conditions are met a violation is created and its actions fire. 1 Indexed artifact component graph, scanned → 2 Watch is it in scope? → 3 Policy + rules conditions met? if a rule matches 4 Violation the record → 5 Actions notify / fail / block The Policy holds the rules; the Watch supplies the scope. You build them in either order, but nothing is enforced until a Watch ties a Policy to a resource. JFrog - Watches in Xray .

title: The three policy types
body: Policy types The three policy types When you create a Policy you pick exactly one type. The type decides which rule conditions you can configure. You cannot mix a license rule into a security policy - you would create one policy of each type and attach both to the same Watch. Type What it catches Typical rule Security Known vulnerabilities (CVEs) and malicious packages in your components. Block anything with a High or Critical CVE that has a fix available. License Compliance Open-source licenses your organization bans or must track. Fail the build if a component uses GPL-3.0. Operational Risk Components that are outdated, deprecated, end-of-life, or poorly maintained. Alert if a dependency has not been updated in 12+ months. Source: JFrog - Create Policies .

title: Writing a security rule
body: Writing rules Writing a security rule A rule is a condition plus the actions to take when it matches. A security policy gives you two ways to define the condition - by how severe the vulnerability is, or by its exact CVSS score. CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) is the industry-standard 0-10 number that rates how dangerous a flaw is; higher is worse. By minimal severity Pick the lowest severity that should trip the rule. Everything at that level or higher matches. Critical High Medium Low All Example: minimal severity = High catches both High and Critical, ignores Medium and below. By CVSS range Set a numeric band, for example CVSS 7.0 to 10.0, when you want finer control than the named buckets give you. Useful when your org's risk threshold sits between two severity labels. Two extra conditions sharpen a security rule so you alert on what is actually actionable: Fix version available. Only flag vulnerabilities that have a released fix, so developers always have somewhere to go. This dramatically cuts noise. Malicious package. Trip on packages JFrog's security research has flagged as deliberately malicious (for example, typosquats), regardless of CVSS. A good first security rule Minimal severity = High , with fix version available turned on, action = create a violation plus notify . It is loud enough to matter, quiet enough to act on, and blocks nothing until you trust it. Tighten to block download later. Source: JFrog - Create Policies (Security Policy Rules) .

title: Writing a license rule
body: Writing rules Writing a license rule A license policy enforces which open-source licenses are allowed in your software. You define the condition as one of two lists. Approach How it works When to use it Banned licenses List the licenses you forbid (e.g. GPL-3.0, AGPL-3.0). A component carrying any of them trips the rule. You only need to block a handful of known-problematic licenses. Allowed licenses List the only licenses you permit (e.g. MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD-3-Clause). Anything outside the list trips the rule. Strict shops that want an explicit allow-list - safer by default. Why a single component can carry several licenses Open-source packages are sometimes dual-licensed or bundle code under more than one license. Xray evaluates every license it detects on a component, so a rule can trip on one license even when others are fine. Build your list with that in mind. Source: JFrog - Policy and Governance (license rules) .

title: Operational-risk rules
body: Writing rules Operational-risk rules Operational risk is about maintainability , not security - components that are stale or abandoned are a liability even when no CVE exists yet. JFrog's documented examples make the intent concrete: Staleness. Alert developers if a dependency has not been updated in 12+ months. End-of-life. Fail builds if a package is flagged as end-of-life. Deprecation. Block downloads of deprecated components. Source: JFrog - Create Policies (Operational Risk Rule) .

title: What a rule can do: enforcement actions
body: Enforcement What a rule can do: enforcement actions Each rule, when matched, always records a violation . On top of that you choose any number of automatic actions. They fall into two families: notify (tell someone) and block (stop the artifact from moving). Action Family Effect Generate a violation Always on The record that something broke a rule. Everything else is optional on top. Notify watch recipients / developer / email list Notify Email the watch owner, the resource's developer, or a configured address list. Trigger a webhook Notify Call an external endpoint (Slack, a pipeline, your own service) when the rule is violated. Create a Jira ticket Notify Open a tracking ticket automatically for each violation. Fail builds Block Make a CI build fail when the rule is violated. Supports a grace period. Block downloads Block Stop the artifact from being downloaded from Artifactory. Supports a grace period. Block Release Bundle promotion / distribution Block Stop a Release Bundle from being promoted or distributed in the release lifecycle. Supports a grace period. Grace periods: how to roll out blocking without an outage The three blocking actions let you set a grace period - a window where the violation is recorded and teams are warned, but the block does not yet bite. This is the safe way to introduce enforcement: start in notify-only, switch on blocking with a grace period, then let the grace period expire once teams have caught up. JFrog - Create Policies (enforcement actions) .

title: Watches: choosing the scope
body: Scope Watches: choosing the scope A Watch answers one question: which resources do these policies apply to? When you add resources to a Watch you choose from four kinds of target. A Watch must have at least one policy attached, or it enforces nothing. Resource What it scopes Example Repositories Everything stored in one or more repos. Watch docker-prod-local for Critical CVEs. Builds The artifacts and dependencies captured in named builds. Watch the payments-api build to gate CI. Release Bundles The signed, immutable bundles you promote and distribute. Block distribution of a bundle with a vulnerable base layer. Projects All resources grouped under a JFrog Project. Apply one compliance baseline across a whole team's project. Best practice: one Watch per environment JFrog recommends separate Watches for different environments - a permissive, notify-only Watch on dev repos and a strict, blocking Watch on production repos - so the same finding is handled appropriately for where it lives. JFrog - Create Watches (best practices) .

title: Lab A: create a security policy
body: Lab A Lab A: create a security policy Goal: a policy that records and notifies on High/Critical vulnerabilities that have a fix, blocking nothing yet. This is the exact click-path from the JFrog docs. 1 Open Watches & Policies. In the JFrog Platform, go to Xray → Watches & Policies . 2 New Policy. Click New Policy and enter a name, for example prod-security-baseline . Add a short description of its purpose. 3 Pick the type. Choose Security Policy . 4 Add a rule. Click Add Rule . Set the rule category to By Minimal Severity = High , and enable Fix version available so you only flag actionable findings. 5 Set actions. Leave the default violation on, and add Notify the watch recipient . Do not enable blocking yet - you will tighten later. 6 Save. Save the policy. It now exists but enforces nothing until a Watch uses it - that is Lab B. Source: JFrog - Create Policies .

title: Lab B: create a watch and attach the policy
body: Lab B Lab B: create a watch and attach the policy Goal: point the policy from Lab A at a real repository so findings start becoming violations. 1 New Watch. Still under Xray → Watches & Policies , click New Watch and give it a unique name, for example prod-repos-watch . 2 Add resources. Click Add Resources and select what to monitor - a Repository (e.g. your production Docker repo), a Build , a Release Bundle , or a Project . Click Apply . 3 Attach the policy. Link the prod-security-baseline policy from Lab A. A Watch can carry several policies - add a license policy here too if you have one. 4 Save & apply. Save the Watch. Xray evaluates the in-scope resources against the attached policies and starts generating violations on matches. 5 Verify. Open the Watch and review its violations. Seeing the High/Critical findings you expected confirms the policy and scope line up. Reminder you will thank yourself for If you created the policy but see no violations, check that a Watch actually attaches it to the right resource. A policy with no Watch is inert by design. Source: JFrog - Create Watches .

title: Recap
body: Recap Policy = rules, Watch = scope. Nothing is enforced until a Watch attaches a Policy to a resource; the result of a match is a violation . Three policy types. Security (CVEs, malicious), License (banned or allowed lists), Operational risk (stale, EOL, deprecated). One type per policy; attach several policies to one Watch. Sharpen security rules with minimal severity or a CVSS range, plus fix-available to cut noise. Actions are notify (email, webhook, Jira) or block (fail build, block download, block bundle promotion). Blocking actions support a grace period for safe rollout. Watches target repositories, builds, release bundles, or projects. Use one Watch per environment.
